SLACK DM — Mike Torres to David Okafor — 5:10 PM

[Mike Torres]
Hey, random thought — you mentioned you dealt with JWT + Redis blacklist at DataScale. What was the final architecture you ended up with there?

[David Okafor]
We eventually ripped out JWT entirely and went with Redis-backed sessions. Similar to what you're alluding to. The JWT blacklist was a maintenance nightmare — we had a bug where the blacklist TTL didn't match the token TTL, so revoked tokens would fall out of the blacklist before they expired. 3-month security incident.

[Mike Torres]
That's exactly the kind of failure mode I'm worried about. Two systems that need to agree on TTLs. How was Redis session performance at DataScale?

[David Okafor]
Excellent. Sub-millisecond lookups. We were doing 50K requests/sec at peak and Redis wasn't even close to breaking a sweat. The real benefit was operational simplicity — revocation was just DELETE, cleanup was built-in via EXPIRE, and failover was handled by Redis Sentinel. No custom code for any of it.

[Mike Torres]
What Redis deployment topology did you use? Single primary with read replicas, or Sentinel, or Cluster?

[David Okafor]
Sentinel with one primary and two replicas. For sessions, you don't need Cluster — the data volume is tiny. Even at 100K concurrent sessions at 500 bytes each, that's only 50MB. Redis handles that on a micro instance. Sentinel gives you automatic failover, which was the main concern. Failover typically completed in 10-15 seconds, during which reads still worked from replicas. Only writes (new logins) would fail briefly.

[Mike Torres]
That lines up with what I'm thinking. Going to benchmark a Redis session store this weekend and write up the proposal.

[David Okafor]
One thing I'd recommend — benchmark not just read/write latency but also the Django integration. We used `django-redis` as the session backend and it was mostly plug-and-play. The `SESSION_ENGINE` setting just points to the Redis backend and everything else (session middleware, CSRF, etc.) works as-is. The migration was surprisingly undramatic.

[Mike Torres]
Oh that's encouraging. I was worried about the Django integration being a heavy lift. If it's just a settings change, that significantly reduces the implementation risk.

[David Okafor]
Yeah, the Django session framework abstracts the backend really well. The main work is standing up and operating Redis itself — provisioning, monitoring, alerting, backup. The application code changes are minimal.

[Mike Torres]
Good to know. I'll include the Django integration details in the ADR.

[David Okafor]
Let me know if you want to pair on it. I've got the DataScale implementation patterns still in my head.

[Mike Torres]
Will do. Thanks.

This is useful because it gives us a leading indicator. If we see a customer's dashboard views drop by more than 40% month-over-month, there's a 78% chance they'll churn within 60 days.

3. Day 14 (if still declining): CS manager escalation. Personal phone call.
4. Day 21 (if still declining): Retention offer — 20% discount for 3 months if they commit to annual.

Categories 1 and 2 are self-service candidates. If users could diagnose their own data source issues and find dashboard how-tos, we'd deflect roughly 55% of tickets.

My proposal: invest in two self-service initiatives.

**Initiative A: Interactive troubleshooting wizard for data sources.**
When a data source sync fails, instead of showing a generic "Sync failed" error, show a guided troubleshooter that walks the user through common fixes — check credentials, check firewall rules, verify the database is reachable, test the connection. The wizard covers the 8 most common failure modes which account for 85% of data source tickets.

I've mapped out the 8 failure modes based on the ticket data:
1. Expired or rotated credentials (24% of data source tickets)
2. Firewall/IP whitelist not configured (19%)
3. Database host unreachable / DNS resolution failure (14%)
4. SSL certificate mismatch (11%)
5. Insufficient database permissions (9%)
6. Connection timeout — network latency or wrong port (8%)
7. Schema changes — table or column renamed/deleted (6%)
8. Rate limiting on the source API (4%)

For each of these, we can run a diagnostic check in the background and present the user with a specific, actionable error message and fix instructions. Instead of "Sync failed — Error 1045," we'd show "Your database credentials appear to be expired. Here's how to update them in Vexflow" with a direct link to the credentials settings page.

**Initiative B: In-app contextual help for dashboard features.**
When a user hovers over or clicks a dashboard feature (like a chart type selector or filter configuration), show a contextual help panel with examples, common configurations, and links to detailed documentation. This overlaps with the inline tips we're adding to the onboarding flow — same design pattern, extended to the full product.

The idea is that instead of leaving the product to search the help center, the user gets a small panel that shows: (1) a brief description of the feature, (2) a screenshot or animation of a common configuration, (3) a link to the full docs if they need more detail. We'd track which help panels are opened most frequently to prioritize content creation.

Estimated impact:
- Initiative A: deflect 200 tickets/month (17% of total), save ~$9,000/month in support costs
- Initiative B: deflect 140 tickets/month (12% of total), save ~$6,300/month in support costs
- Combined: 29% ticket reduction, $15,300/month savings

ITEM 1: EXPORT PIPELINE REVIEW (2:00–2:22 PM)

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:00 PM]
Alright, let's get started. We've got a packed agenda today so I want to keep us on track. Sarah, you're up first with the export pipeline.

[Sarah Kim — 2:01 PM]
Thanks Priya. Export pipeline v2 is feature-complete. PR #1863 has the full implementation — it's a big PR, about 2,400 lines across 18 files, but most of that is test coverage. Quick rundown of what shipped:

- Streaming S3 multipart upload — tested up to 6GB exports with flat memory usage (~190MB peak)
- Retry logic: exponential backoff with jitter, max 3 retries per chunk, dead-letter queue for permanently failed exports
- Progress tracking via WebSocket push (thanks Rachel for the WS infrastructure)
- Per-tenant size limits: Free tier 100MB, Pro tier 1GB, Enterprise tier 10GB
- Email notification on completion with a signed download link (24-hour expiry)
- CSV format for v1, with a clean interface for adding Parquet later

The worker runs as a separate ECS task with its own autoscaling group. It scales based on the export job queue depth — 1 worker per 5 pending jobs, max 10 workers.

[Tomás Herrera — 2:04 PM]
Sarah, quick clarification on the multipart upload — what chunk size are you using for the S3 parts? And are you doing the checksums on each part or just the final object?

[Sarah Kim — 2:04 PM]
Good question. Chunk size is 8MB, which is the sweet spot between memory usage and upload parallelism. Each part gets a SHA-256 checksum via the `ChecksumSHA256` parameter in the `upload_part` call — S3 validates it server-side. The final `complete_multipart_upload` also gets a composite checksum. So we get end-to-end integrity verification.

[Tomás Herrera — 2:05 PM]
Nice. That's cleaner than the approach I used on the data import pipeline. I might borrow that pattern.

[David Okafor — 2:05 PM]
I reviewed the PR. The streaming implementation is solid. One question about the signed download links — are they tenant-scoped? Meaning, can a user from Tenant A use a download link generated for Tenant B?

[Sarah Kim — 2:06 PM]
No. The signed URL is generated with the tenant_id in the path prefix, and the S3 bucket policy requires the IAM role to match the tenant. Even if someone gets the URL, the IAM validation would fail. Defense in depth.

The S3 key structure is: `exports/{tenant_id}/{export_id}/{filename}.csv`. The bucket policy restricts the `exports/{tenant_id}/*` prefix to the IAM role associated with that tenant. So even with a valid presigned URL, the request would fail at the IAM level if the calling role doesn't match.

[Nadia Volkov — 2:07 PM]
Do we log when download links are generated and when they're actually used? For the audit trail.

[Sarah Kim — 2:07 PM]
Yes — link generation is logged in the `export_events` table with the user_id, tenant_id, export_id, and timestamp. The download itself is tracked via S3 access logs, which we ship to CloudWatch. I added a CloudWatch Insights query in the runbook for correlating the two.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:07 PM]
Good. What's the rollout plan?

[Sarah Kim — 2:08 PM]
I want to do a staged rollout. First, deploy to staging and have the QA team run the test matrix — small exports (1MB), medium (100MB), large (2GB), and an edge case with empty datasets. Then deploy to production behind a feature flag for internal tenants only. After 48 hours of monitoring, open it to Pro and Enterprise tiers. Free tier gets it the following week.

I've already drafted the test matrix. The edge cases include: empty dataset (0 rows), single row, dataset with NULL values in every column, dataset with Unicode characters in column names, and a dataset that hits exactly the per-tier size limit.

[Mike Torres — 2:09 PM]
What happens when a Free tier user tries to export something that's 150MB? Do they get an error at the start or does it fail partway through?

[Sarah Kim — 2:09 PM]
It checks the estimated size before starting the export. The estimate is based on the row count and average row size from `pg_stats`. If the estimate exceeds the tier limit, the user gets a 413 with a clear message: "Export size (~150MB) exceeds your plan limit (100MB). Upgrade to Pro for up to 1GB exports." If the estimate is under but the actual export goes over — which can happen if the stats are stale — it aborts the export and returns a similar error. No partial files left behind.

[James Holden — 2:10 PM]

Added a section to the export docs — export files have a 30-day lifecycle policy independent of source data retention. Also added a warning banner in the UI: "Export files are available for 30 days." It's documented in the API docs too.

[Anika Patel — 2:11 PM]
Is the 30-day lifecycle managed by an S3 lifecycle rule, or do we have our own cleanup job?

[Sarah Kim — 2:11 PM]
S3 lifecycle rule. It's the simplest approach and it's infrastructure-level — no code to maintain, no cron job to monitor. The rule is defined in the Terraform config for the exports bucket. Objects in the `exports/` prefix get `Expiration: Days=30`. S3 handles the rest.

ITEM 2: WEBSOCKET MIGRATION UPDATE (2:22–2:38 PM)

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:22 PM]
WebSocket migration is making progress but I hit a couple snags. Let me update.

Good news: the core WebSocket connection is working end-to-end in staging. Clients connect, subscribe to channels, receive real-time updates. The export progress tracking that Sarah mentioned is the first consumer and it works great.

The ALB issue I mentioned last week — Carlos and I dug into it. The problem was that our ALB was configured with the default idle timeout of 60 seconds, which was killing WebSocket connections during quiet periods. We bumped it to 3600 seconds and the drops stopped. Also switched from round-robin to least-connections for the WS target group.

[Tomás Herrera — 2:25 PM]
Rachel, what library are you using for the WS server? And are you handling the ping/pong frames for keepalive?

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:25 PM]
We're using `websockets` (the Python library) behind uvicorn. For keepalive, the server sends a ping frame every 30 seconds. If it doesn't get a pong within 10 seconds, it considers the connection dead and cleans up. On the client side, the JS reconnection logic uses exponential backoff — 1s, 2s, 4s, 8s, max 30s between attempts.

[Lin Zhang — 2:26 PM]
The client-side reconnection is already in the frontend PR I put up last week — `useWebSocket` hook with automatic reconnect and an `onReconnect` callback so components can refresh their state after a dropped connection.

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:27 PM]
Yeah, Lin's hook has been great for the frontend integration. We tested the reconnection flow by killing WS processes on staging and the clients reconnected within 2-3 seconds consistently.

The snags:
1. **Connection auth** — currently the WS handshake uses the session cookie for auth, same as HTTP requests. This works but it means the WS connection lifetime is tied to the session lifetime. If the session expires while a WS connection is open, we need to either (a) close the connection and force re-auth, or (b) extend the session. Right now we do nothing and the connection stays open but unauthenticated. That's a security gap.

2. **Scale concern** — each WS connection holds a server-side state (the socket, the subscriptions). At 500 concurrent users with an average of 2 WS connections each, that's 1000 persistent connections across our 3 API servers. Current memory overhead is about 2KB per connection, so ~2MB total. That's fine now but if we get to 5000 users it's 10K connections and 20MB. Still manageable but we'd want a dedicated WS tier at that point.

[Nadia Volkov — 2:29 PM]
On the scale concern — are the subscriptions stored in memory or in Redis? If a WS server crashes, do clients need to re-subscribe?

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:29 PM]
In memory currently. If the server crashes, clients reconnect (Lin's hook handles that) and re-subscribe. The subscribe message is idempotent. For pub/sub between servers, I'm using Redis PUB/SUB so that a message published by one server reaches all connected clients regardless of which server they're connected to. But the subscription state itself is per-process.

[Mike Torres — 2:30 PM]
Point 1 is interesting. It ties back to the auth architecture discussion. If we go with JWT, the WS handshake would validate a token and the connection would be independent of any server-side session. The token could expire and the connection would still be valid (or not — your choice). If we go with server-side sessions, we'd want to keep the session alive as long as the WS connection is alive, which means the WS server needs to be able to touch the session store.

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:31 PM]
Yeah, I was thinking about that. For now I'm going to implement the "close and re-auth" approach — when the session nears expiry, send a WS message telling the client to re-authenticate. That works regardless of the session backend.

The message format will be something like:

```json
{
  "type": "auth_expiring",
  "expires_in_seconds": 60,
  "action": "re_authenticate"
}
```

The client gets 60 seconds to refresh its auth and re-establish the WS connection. If it doesn't, the server closes the connection with close code 4001 (auth expired).

[Sarah Kim — 2:32 PM]
Smart. Keep it backend-agnostic until the ADR is resolved.

[Carlos Rivera — 2:33 PM]
On the infrastructure side, I've set up a separate target group for WS connections on port 8001. The API servers run both HTTP (8000) and WS (8001) but the ALB routes them separately. This gives us the option to split WS to dedicated instances later without changing the client.

The ALB listener rules are:
- `/ws/*` → target group `rivulet-ws-tg` (port 8001, sticky sessions disabled)
- `/*` → target group `rivulet-api-tg` (port 8000)

Health checks for the WS target group hit a dedicated `/ws/health` endpoint that verifies the WS server is accepting connections.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:34 PM]
Good forward-thinking. Rachel, what's left for the WS migration?

[Rachel Fernandez — 2:35 PM]

ITEM 3: AUTH ARCHITECTURE UPDATE (2:38–2:55 PM)

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:38 PM]
OK, auth update. Sarah and Mike, where are we on ADR-017?

[Sarah Kim — 2:39 PM]
I've refined the JWT proposal based on Mike's feedback. The main change is I'm now proposing 10-minute access tokens instead of 15, with the refresh token stored in Redis (not PostgreSQL) so the refresh endpoint doesn't hit the main database. This addresses the connection pool concern for the refresh flow.

The access token payload would look like:

```json
{
  "sub": "user_abc123",
  "tid": "tenant_xyz",
  "role": "admin",
  "exp": 1718000000,
  "iat": 1717999400,
  "jti": "unique-token-id"
}
```

The `jti` is stored in a Redis-backed revocation set. On each request, the middleware checks if the `jti` is in the revocation set. If it is, the token is rejected. The revocation set entries have a TTL matching the token expiry, so they auto-clean.

But honestly, after Mike's revocation analysis, I think there's merit to exploring a pure session-token approach. Mike, did you get the benchmark done?

[Mike Torres — 2:40 PM]
I did. David and I looked at this over the weekend. Short version: a dedicated session store with token-based lookup solves all four of our requirements without the JWT revocation complexity.

I'm not going to do a full presentation because we're short on time, but here's the gist: What about session tokens in Redis? You issue an opaque session token — just a random string, say 256 bits — and store the session data in Redis keyed by that token. SET with an EXPIRE for automatic TTL-based cleanup. Lookup is GET — single key lookup, sub-millisecond. Revocation is DEL — delete the key, session is immediately invalid.

The session data structure in Redis:

```
Key:   session:{token_hash}
Value: {"user_id": "abc123", "tenant_id": "xyz", "role": "admin", "created_at": "2025-06-09T14:00:00Z"}
TTL:   3600 (1 hour)
```

I hash the token before using it as the key so that even if someone dumps the Redis keyspace, they can't use the keys as session tokens.

[Sarah Kim — 2:42 PM]
That handles revocation natively. No blacklist, no dual-system problem.

[Mike Torres — 2:42 PM]
Exactly. And the TTL handles cleanup. No cron job, no timezone bugs. Redis does it internally. When the TTL expires, the key vanishes. The session is gone.

Here are the benchmark numbers from Saturday — I spun up a single Redis 7.2 instance (t3.medium equivalent) and ran a load test:
- SET (create session): P50 0.08ms, P99 0.22ms
- GET (lookup session): P50 0.06ms, P99 0.18ms
- DEL (revoke session): P50 0.05ms, P99 0.15ms
- Throughput: 85,000 ops/sec sustained

For comparison, our PostgreSQL session lookup was P50 2.1ms, P99 47ms. Redis is 35x faster at the median and 260x faster at P99. And that's on a single instance — Redis Cluster scales horizontally if we ever need it.

[David Okafor — 2:44 PM]
I can add some context from the database side. The PostgreSQL session table has about 120,000 rows right now, and the lookup query is a simple primary key select. But the P99 spikes happen when the connection pool is saturated — we've got 20 connections in the pool and during peak traffic every connection is busy serving API queries. The session lookup has to wait in the queue. Moving sessions to Redis eliminates that contention entirely because it's a separate connection pool to a separate store.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:45 PM]
Those numbers are compelling. Sarah, thoughts?

[Sarah Kim — 2:46 PM]
The numbers are great. My concern with Redis is operational — it's another piece of infrastructure to manage. We don't run Redis in production today. We'd need to set up the cluster, monitoring, backups, failover... Carlos, what's the effort there?

[Carlos Rivera — 2:47 PM]
Actually, we DO run Redis — ElastiCache, us-east-1. Mike's rate limiter uses it. The rate limiter Redis is a single-node r6g.medium. For sessions, I'd want a multi-AZ cluster with automatic failover — probably a 3-node setup with 1 primary and 2 replicas. ElastiCache makes that pretty straightforward. I could have it provisioned in a day.

The Terraform module for ElastiCache is already set up — I'd just add a second replication group for sessions. The monitoring is also already in place — we've got CloudWatch alarms on memory usage, CPU, replication lag, and eviction count for the rate limiter Redis. I'd clone those for the session cluster.

Cost-wise, three r6g.medium nodes in us-east-1 run about $0.134/hr each, so roughly $290/month total for the session cluster. That's less than what we spend on the PostgreSQL connection pool overhead during peak traffic.

[Sarah Kim — 2:48 PM]
Oh, I forgot about the rate limiter Redis. OK, so we already have the operational muscle for Redis. That addresses my concern.

[Mike Torres — 2:48 PM]
One more thing — token rotation. I'm proposing that every time the session is validated, we issue a new session token and invalidate the old one. This is called token rotation. If someone intercepts a token, it's only valid until the next request from the legitimate user. The old token gets DEL'd and the new one gets SET with a fresh TTL.

The flow looks like:

1. Client sends request with token A
2. Server validates token A (GET session:{hash(A)})
3. Server generates new token B
4. Server creates session:{hash(B)} with same session data and fresh TTL
5. Server deletes session:{hash(A)}
6. Server returns response with new token B in the Set-Cookie header

This is atomic via a Redis MULTI/EXEC transaction so there's no window where both tokens are valid or neither is.

[David Okafor — 2:49 PM]
That's what we did at DataScale. It makes session theft much harder — the attacker and the legitimate user would race, and the first one to use the token invalidates it for the other. If the server sees an already-rotated token, it can flag the session as potentially compromised and force re-authentication.

[Nadia Volkov — 2:50 PM]
Does token rotation play nicely with concurrent requests? If I have two browser tabs open and they both send a request at the same time with token A, one of them would invalidate it before the other completes.

[Mike Torres — 2:50 PM]
Good catch. The standard approach is a grace period — when you rotate from A to B, you keep A valid for an additional 5-10 seconds to handle in-flight requests. After the grace period, A is hard-deleted. I'll spec that out in the ADR.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:51 PM]
OK. We're almost out of time on this item. It sounds like we have two well-understood options. Sarah, Mike — is there a path to convergence here, or do we need to formally vote?

[Mike Torres — 2:51 PM]
I think the tradeoffs are clear. JWT is more elegant architecturally but the revocation problem is real for our enterprise use case. Redis sessions are simpler operationally but require a dedicated store.

[Sarah Kim — 2:52 PM]
I agree the tradeoffs are clear. I'm not going to die on the JWT hill — Mike's revocation analysis is solid. Let's think about it and decide.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 2:53 PM]
Fair enough. Let's both update ADR-017 with the final versions of each proposal and plan to decide this week. I'd like to have this settled before Sprint 25 planning on Monday. Moving on.

ITEM 4: CI/CD PIPELINE IMPROVEMENTS (2:55–3:12 PM)

[Carlos Rivera — 2:55 PM]
Build times. We're at 14 minutes for a full CI run. I've been profiling the pipeline and found three main bottlenecks:

1. **Docker layer caching** — we're not caching intermediate layers between runs. Every build starts from scratch. Fix: use BuildKit with registry-based caching. Estimated savings: 4 minutes.

2. **Test parallelization** — we're running all 847 tests in a single pytest process. Fix: split into 4 parallel shards using pytest-xdist. Estimated savings: 3 minutes.

3. **Dependency installation** — `pip install` runs on every build because the virtual environment isn't cached. Fix: cache the venv directory keyed on requirements.txt hash. Estimated savings: 2 minutes.

Total estimated reduction: 14 minutes → 5 minutes. I've got PRs for all three — #1860 (Docker caching), #1861 (test sharding), #1862 (dependency caching).

Let me walk through each one briefly.

For the Docker caching, the key change is adding `--cache-from` and `--cache-to` flags to our `docker build` command:

```
docker buildx build \
  --cache-from type=registry,ref=our-ecr/rivulet:cache \
  --cache-to type=registry,ref=our-ecr/rivulet:cache,mode=max \
  -t our-ecr/rivulet:$COMMIT_SHA .
```

The `mode=max` ensures all layers are cached, not just the final image layers. This means even if we change the application code, the dependency installation layers are still cached.

[Mike Torres — 2:58 PM]
Nice. The test sharding — does it handle test database fixtures correctly? We have some tests that share database state.

[Carlos Rivera — 2:59 PM]
Good question. I'm using pytest-xdist with `--dist loadgroup`. Tests in the same file run in the same worker, so file-level database fixtures are preserved. Cross-file state sharing doesn't work, but we shouldn't have that anyway. I found 3 tests that were accidentally depending on cross-file state and fixed them in #1861.

Specifically, `test_dashboard_sharing.py` was depending on a user created in `test_user_management.py`, `test_export_cleanup.py` was reading export records created by `test_export_creation.py`, and `test_notification_prefs.py` was depending on a tenant created in `test_tenant_setup.py`. All three now create their own fixtures.

[Tomás Herrera — 3:00 PM]
Were those tests passing before by accident? Like, they relied on test execution order?

[Carlos Rivera — 3:00 PM]
Exactly. pytest happens to run files in alphabetical order by default, so `test_tenant_setup.py` always ran before `test_notification_prefs.py`. But with xdist, the execution order is nondeterministic. That's actually a benefit — it surfaced these hidden dependencies.

[James Holden — 3:00 PM]
What about flaky tests? If we're running in parallel, flaky tests become harder to debug because the failure might be timing-dependent.

[Carlos Rivera — 3:01 PM]
I added pytest-rerunfailures with `--reruns 2`. Flaky tests get retried twice before failing. Not ideal — we should fix flaky tests — but it prevents them from blocking the pipeline.

I also added a flaky test report to the CI output. After each run, it lists any tests that passed on retry. Right now we have 7 tests that have been flaky in the last 30 days. I'll file tickets for each of them.

[Nadia Volkov — 3:02 PM]
For the dependency caching — are you caching the entire venv or just the pip download cache?

[Carlos Rivera — 3:02 PM]
The entire venv. The cache key is the SHA-256 of `requirements.txt` + `requirements-dev.txt` + the Python version. If any of those change, the cache misses and we do a full install. Otherwise, we restore the cached venv and skip the install entirely. In practice, we change dependencies maybe once a sprint, so 90%+ of builds will hit the cache.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 3:03 PM]
Good. Let's review and merge those this week. 5-minute builds would be a big quality-of-life improvement. Carlos, can you tag Tomás and Nadia as reviewers on those PRs?

[Carlos Rivera — 3:03 PM]
Already done. Tomás has #1860, Nadia has #1862, and James has #1861.

ITEM 5: MULTI-WORKSPACE SCOPING (3:12–3:42 PM)

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 3:12 PM]
Last item. Product confirmed multi-workspace is the top Q3 priority. The Meridian Health deal depends on it — they need separate workspaces for each hospital by August. I want to start the technical scoping now.

The basic requirement: a single tenant (Meridian Health) can have multiple workspaces (Hospital A, Hospital B, etc.). Each workspace has:
- Its own set of data sources and datasets
- Its own dashboards and reports
- Its own user permissions (a user can have admin in Workspace A but viewer in Workspace B)
- Data isolation — queries in Workspace A cannot see Workspace B's data

I circulated RFC-042 last week with the product requirements. David, I know you've been looking at the data model — can you walk us through the impact?

[David Okafor — 3:15 PM]
This is a significant data model change. Every major table needs a workspace_id column. Let me list the impact:
- `events` table — 48M rows, needs workspace_id + index rebuild
- `dashboards` — needs workspace_id
- `data_sources` — needs workspace_id
- `export_jobs` — needs workspace_id (Sarah, your export pipeline will need this)
- `permissions` — needs to become workspace-scoped
- All API endpoints need workspace context (either URL prefix or header)

The events table migration is the scary one. 48M rows, adding a non-nullable column with a foreign key. We'd need to do it as a multi-step migration: add nullable column, backfill, add NOT NULL constraint, add index. Probably 2-3 hours of migration time on production.

I've sketched out the migration sequence:

Step 1 (instant): `ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN workspace_id UUID;` — nullable, no default, no lock
Step 2 (hours): Batched UPDATE in a background job — 10,000 rows per batch, 100ms sleep between batches. All existing rows get the default workspace ID for their tenant.
Step 3 (seconds): `ALTER TABLE events ALTER COLUMN workspace_id SET NOT NULL;` with `NOT VALID` — deferred validation
Step 4 (minutes): `ALTER TABLE events VALIDATE CONSTRAINT events_workspace_id_not_null;` — full scan, but no lock
Step 5 (minutes): `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_events_workspace_id ON events (workspace_id);`

[Sarah Kim — 3:18 PM]
Can we do the migration online with zero downtime? pg_repack or something similar?

[David Okafor — 3:19 PM]
For the column addition, yes — PostgreSQL can add a nullable column without locking. The backfill can run as a batched UPDATE in a background job. The NOT NULL constraint needs a full table scan to validate but with the `NOT VALID` option we can defer that. The index creation can use `CONCURRENTLY`. So yes, zero downtime is achievable but it's a multi-day rollout.

The risk is the backfill step. At 10K rows per batch with a 100ms pause, 48M rows takes about 80 minutes. During that time, we need the application to handle rows that have workspace_id and rows that don't. The API layer would need to coalesce NULL workspace_id to the default workspace for the tenant.

[Tomás Herrera — 3:20 PM]
Have you considered doing the backfill at the read layer instead? Like, a database view that returns the default workspace_id for NULL rows? That way the application doesn't need the coalescing logic.

[David Okafor — 3:20 PM]
I considered that but views add a query planning overhead. For 48M rows, I'd rather do the backfill once and be done. The coalescing logic is a single COALESCE in the query, and we'd remove it once the backfill is complete.

[Lin Zhang — 3:21 PM]
The frontend impact is also significant. Every page that shows data needs to be workspace-aware. The sidebar navigation needs a workspace switcher. The URL structure changes — probably `/workspaces/{id}/dashboards` instead of just `/dashboards`.

The workspace switcher would be a dropdown in the top nav, next to the user avatar. When you switch workspaces, the entire page context reloads — new dashboards, new data sources, new permissions. I'd implement it as a React context provider at the app root, and all data-fetching hooks would include the workspace_id from context.

[Anika Patel — 3:22 PM]
The onboarding flow I just rewrote needs to handle workspace creation too. Right now it creates a tenant and you're done. With workspaces, we'd need a step to create the first workspace, or auto-create a default workspace.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 3:23 PM]
I think the default workspace approach is right. When a new tenant signs up, we auto-create a "Default" workspace. They can create additional workspaces later. This way the migration is also simpler — all existing data goes into the default workspace.

[Nadia Volkov — 3:24 PM]
For the data isolation — are we doing row-level security in PostgreSQL, or is it purely application-enforced?

[David Okafor — 3:24 PM]
Application-enforced for now. Every query includes a `WHERE workspace_id = :workspace_id` clause. We already do this for tenant_id, so it's the same pattern — just one more filter. Row-level security is an option for the future but it adds complexity to the migration and to debugging queries.

[Mike Torres — 3:25 PM]
What about the session model? Currently a session is tenant-scoped. With workspaces, does a session need to carry workspace context? Like, when I'm logged in, am I "in" a specific workspace?

[Rachel Fernandez — 3:26 PM]
I'd argue the session is still tenant-scoped. The workspace is a client-side context — you switch workspaces in the UI, and the client sends the workspace_id with each request. The server validates that the user has permission in that workspace but the session itself is just "this user is authenticated for this tenant."

[Mike Torres — 3:27 PM]
That works. The session stores user_id and tenant_id. The workspace context is per-request, not per-session. Keeps the auth layer clean.

[James Holden — 3:27 PM]
What about API keys? Some of our Enterprise customers use API keys for automation. Are API keys workspace-scoped or tenant-scoped?

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 3:28 PM]
Good question. I'd say workspace-scoped. An API key should only have access to one workspace. If you need to automate across workspaces, you create separate keys. That's more secure and it's consistent with how our competitors handle it.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 3:28 PM]
Good. So the auth changes for multi-workspace are minimal — just adding workspace-level permission checks on top of the existing tenant-level auth. That's a separate concern from the session backend discussion.

Let me summarize the scoping:
- **Database**: Multi-step migration, 2-3 weeks including the events table backfill
- **Backend API**: Workspace-aware endpoints, permission middleware, ~40 endpoints affected
- **Frontend**: Workspace switcher, URL restructure, workspace-scoped views
- **Infrastructure**: No new infrastructure needed (workspace is a logical partition, not physical)

Estimated total effort: 6-8 weeks. Which means we need to start Sprint 25 at the latest.

DECISIONS LOG

1. **We decided to require design reviews for tickets estimated at 5+ story points** before sprint commitment. Lightweight design doc (1 page) reviewed by at least one other engineer. Format: problem statement, proposed approach, known risks, open questions. Doc goes on Confluence, linked from Jira ticket.

2. **We decided to adopt mandatory pairing for infrastructure and data pipeline tickets.** Optional for feature work. Infrastructure defined as: databases, caching, deployment configuration, monitoring, shared libraries — anything other services depend on. Pairing sessions scheduled within the 10am–3pm CT overlap window.

3. **We decided to enforce 48-hour post-incident reviews for P2+ incidents.** Reviews block non-emergency deployments until completed. Deployment block applies team-wide to create collective ownership. Template: timeline, root cause (five-whys), impact, action items, owner. Target: 30 minutes per review.

4. **We decided to enforce formal trade-off discussions for all mid-sprint scope additions.** Must identify what's being dropped. P1 exempt, P2 expedited (same-day decision). Scope changes logged on the sprint board for trend tracking.

## Set up Redis session store with token rotation

### Summary

Implements a Redis-backed session store to replace the current PostgreSQL `django_session` backend. Sessions are stored as Redis keys with built-in TTL for automatic expiry. Includes token rotation on each request for security hardening.

### Background

Our PostgreSQL session store is causing user-facing issues (SUP-4398–4402, SUP-4421, SUP-4423):
- Connection pool contention during peak load causes session lookup timeouts
- Session cleanup cron job had a timezone bug (fixed in #1858) but the cron-based approach is fragile
- Session table (15K+ rows) competing with application queries for pool connections

See ADR-017 for full analysis.

### What This PR Does

**New session middleware** (`rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py`):
- Replaces `django.contrib.sessions.middleware.SessionMiddleware`
- On each request: reads session token from cookie, performs Redis GET to retrieve session data
- If session is valid: deserializes session data, attaches `user_id` and `tenant_id` to request, rotates token
- If session is invalid/expired: returns 401 (API) or redirects to login (browser)
- Token rotation: generates new 256-bit token, SET with TTL, DEL old token — atomic via Lua script
- Exempted paths configurable via `SESSION_EXEMPT_PATHS` (defaults to `/api/health`, `/api/auth/login`, `/api/auth/signup`)
- Middleware ordering: runs after `CorsMiddleware`, before `TenantContextMiddleware`

**Session manager** (`rivulet/auth/session_manager.py`):
- `create_session(user_id, tenant_id, metadata)` → creates Redis entry, returns session token
- `get_session(token)` → retrieves session data, returns None if expired/missing
- `rotate_token(old_token)` → atomic rotate: generate new token, copy data, set TTL, delete old
- `revoke_session(token)` → DEL the key. Immediate invalidation.
- `revoke_all_user_sessions(user_id)` → scans for all sessions for a user, DELs them all
- `get_active_session_count(user_id)` → returns number of active sessions for a user
- All public methods log structured events via `structlog` with `action`, `user_id`, `session_token_prefix` (first 8 chars only)

**Redis connection management** (`rivulet/auth/redis_pool.py`):
- Dedicated connection pool for session operations (separate from rate limiter pool)
- Pool size: 20 connections (configurable via `SESSION_REDIS_POOL_SIZE`)
- Connection timeout: 100ms (fast-fail if Redis is unresponsive)
- Retry: 1 retry with 50ms backoff
- Health check interval: 30 seconds (`health_check_interval=30` on the pool)
- Socket keepalive enabled to prevent idle connection drops behind NAT/firewalls

**Token format**:
- 256-bit random bytes, base64url-encoded → 43 characters
- Prefix: `rsess_` for easy identification in logs
- Example: `rsess_a7Kx9mP2qL4nR8vT1wB5yC3dF6hJ0sU2eG4iM7o`
- Redis key format: `session:{token}` with configurable key prefix
- Token generation uses `secrets.token_urlsafe(32)` — cryptographically secure

**Session data structure** (stored as JSON in Redis):
```json
{
  "user_id": "usr_a1b2c3d4",
  "tenant_id": "tnt_x9y8z7w6",
  "created_at": "2025-06-11T10:30:00Z",
  "last_rotated_at": "2025-06-11T10:45:00Z",
  "ip_address": "192.168.1.100",
  "user_agent": "Mozilla/5.0...",
  "metadata": {}
}
```

**Session lifecycle**:
- Default TTL: 24 hours (configurable via `SESSION_TTL_SECONDS`)
- Token rotation resets TTL → active users never expire
- Idle sessions expire naturally via Redis TTL
- Max sessions per user: 10 (configurable via `SESSION_MAX_PER_USER`) — creating an 11th revokes the oldest

**Security features**:
- Token rotation on every request (stolen tokens are single-use)
- Duplicate token detection: if a rotated-out token is used, all sessions for that user are revoked (indicates possible token theft)
- Session binding: IP address and user agent are stored and optionally validated (strict mode for enterprise)
- Rate limiting on login endpoint: 5 attempts per minute per IP (uses existing rate limiter)
- Tokens never appear in logs — only the first 8 characters are logged (e.g., `rsess_a7K*****`)

### Configuration

```python
# settings.py additions
SESSION_BACKEND = "rivulet.auth.session_middleware.RedisSessionMiddleware"
SESSION_REDIS_URL = env("SESSION_REDIS_URL", default="redis://localhost:6379/1")
SESSION_REDIS_POOL_SIZE = env.int("SESSION_REDIS_POOL_SIZE", default=20)
SESSION_TTL_SECONDS = env.int("SESSION_TTL_SECONDS", default=86400)  # 24 hours
SESSION_MAX_PER_USER = env.int("SESSION_MAX_PER_USER", default=10)
SESSION_ROTATE_TOKENS = env.bool("SESSION_ROTATE_TOKENS", default=True)
SESSION_STRICT_BINDING = env.bool("SESSION_STRICT_BINDING", default=False)
SESSION_EXEMPT_PATHS = env.list("SESSION_EXEMPT_PATHS", default=[
    "/api/health",
    "/api/auth/login",
    "/api/auth/signup",
    "/api/auth/forgot-password",
])
```

### Migration Plan

Phase 1 (this PR): Deploy the Redis session middleware behind a feature flag. New logins get Redis sessions. Existing PostgreSQL sessions continue to work via the fallback path in the middleware.

Phase 2 (follow-up PR): After 14 days (max existing session age), remove the PostgreSQL session fallback. Delete the `django_session` table. Remove the `clearsessions` cron job.

Phase 3 (follow-up PR): Add admin API for session management — list active sessions, force revoke, session audit log. This is needed for the SOC 2 audit trail.

### Infrastructure Requirements

- Redis 7.x instance for sessions (separate from rate limiter Redis)
- Recommended: ElastiCache Multi-AZ with automatic failover
- Memory estimate: 1KB per session × 15K active sessions = ~15MB (well within t3.medium)
- Carlos has confirmed he can provision the ElastiCache cluster this week

### Lua Scripts

Token rotation is implemented as an atomic Lua script to prevent race conditions:

```lua
-- rotate_token.lua
-- Atomically rotate a session token
-- KEYS[1] = old session key
-- KEYS[2] = new session key
-- ARGV[1] = TTL in seconds

local data = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])
if not data then
    return nil  -- old token already expired/revoked
end

-- Set new key with data and TTL
redis.call('SET', KEYS[2], data, 'EX', ARGV[1])
-- Delete old key
redis.call('DEL', KEYS[1])
-- Track rotation for duplicate detection
redis.call('SET', KEYS[1] .. ':rotated', KEYS[2], 'EX', 300)

return data
```

The `:rotated` suffix key is used for duplicate detection. If a request comes in with a token that has been rotated out, we check if the `:rotated` key exists. If it does, someone is using an old token — possible session theft. We revoke all sessions for that user as a precaution.

**Duplicate detection script** (`duplicate_check.lua`):
```lua
-- duplicate_check.lua
-- Check if a token was previously rotated out
-- KEYS[1] = old session key + ':rotated'
-- Returns: the new token key if rotated, nil otherwise

local rotated_to = redis.call('GET', KEYS[1])
if rotated_to then
    -- Extract user_id from the new session to revoke all
    local new_data = redis.call('GET', rotated_to)
    return new_data
end
return nil
```

### Rollback Plan

If Redis sessions cause issues in staging or production:
1. Set feature flag `redis_sessions` to OFF — all new logins go back to Django sessions
2. Existing Redis sessions will still work until they expire (24h max)
3. No data migration needed — PostgreSQL session table is untouched during Phase 1

### Docker Compose Changes

Added a `session-redis` service for local development:

```yaml
# docker-compose.yml additions
session-redis:
  image: redis:7.2-alpine
  ports:
    - "6380:6379"  # 6380 to avoid conflict with rate-limiter redis on 6379
  volumes:
    - session-redis-data:/data
  command: redis-server --appendonly yes --maxmemory 256mb --maxmemory-policy volatile-lru
  healthcheck:
    test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
    interval: 10s
    timeout: 5s
    retries: 3
```

Note: local dev uses port 6380 mapped to container 6379. The default `SESSION_REDIS_URL` in `settings/local.py` is `redis://localhost:6380/1`.

REVIEW COMMENTS

[Sarah Kim — @sarahkim — 11:23 AM]
This is clean. A few comments:

**rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py, line 47:**
The fallback to PostgreSQL sessions — is this synchronous? If Redis is down and we fall back to PostgreSQL, we're back to the connection pool problem for those requests.

**rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py, line 83:**
Minor: the `_is_exempt_path` check does a linear scan through the exempt paths list. That's fine for 4 entries, but since this runs on every request, a set would be more appropriate if the list grows. Not blocking.

**rivulet/auth/session_manager.py, line 112:**
`revoke_all_user_sessions` uses SCAN to find all sessions for a user. SCAN is O(N) over the keyspace. At 15K sessions this is fine, but at 100K+ it could be slow. Should we maintain a user→sessions index?

**rivulet/auth/session_manager.py, line 45:**
The `create_session` method — I like that you're using `SET ... NX` to prevent token collisions. The probability of a collision with 256-bit tokens is astronomically low, but defense in depth is always good.

**General:**
The code quality is high. Tests are thorough. The Lua script for atomic rotation is the right approach. I'm approving with the two comments above as non-blocking suggestions.

I also ran through the test suite locally on my machine — all 225 tests pass (including the 38 new ones). The `conftest.py` fixtures for spinning up a local Redis instance via `fakeredis` are well done. Easy to run without Docker.

Approval: **Approved** :white_check_mark:

[David Okafor — @dokafor — 12:05 PM]
Reviewed. This matches the Redis session pattern we used at DataScale almost exactly (which makes sense since we discussed it). The duplicate token detection via `:rotated` keys is clever — we didn't have that at DataScale and it would have caught the session theft incident we had in 2023.

**rivulet/auth/redis_pool.py, line 23:**
The 100ms connection timeout is aggressive. If there's a brief Redis hiccup (GC pause, failover), we'll fast-fail and return 401s. Consider 250ms with 1 retry. In my experience, Redis GC pauses are typically 50-150ms.

**rivulet/auth/redis_pool.py, line 38:**
On the retry logic — the 50ms backoff is fine for a single retry, but if we ever bump this to 2 retries, we should switch to exponential backoff. Just a note for future-proofing.

**rivulet/auth/session_manager.py, line 112:**
Agree with Sarah on the SCAN concern. A sorted set `user_sessions:{user_id}` with session tokens as members and creation time as score would make `revoke_all_user_sessions` O(M) where M is the number of sessions for that user, not O(N) over all sessions. Add it now or as a follow-up?

**rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py, line 102:**
I notice the middleware logs the full user agent string. That's fine for debugging but those strings can be 200+ characters. Consider truncating to 128 chars in the log output to keep structured log entries from getting too wide. The full string is still stored in the session data for auditing.

**rivulet/auth/token_utils.py, line 15:**
The `generate_token` function — worth adding a brief docstring explaining why we use `secrets.token_urlsafe` instead of `uuid4`. (Answer: `uuid4` is only 122 bits of randomness, `secrets.token_urlsafe(32)` gives 256 bits.)

**tests/auth/test_token_rotation.py, line 89:**
The concurrent rotation test is good. I especially like the `test_concurrent_rotation_same_token` test that verifies exactly one rotation wins and the other gets a clean `None` response. Edge case that's easy to miss.

Approval: **Approved** :white_check_mark:

[Rachel Wu — @rwu — 12:18 PM]
Not a required reviewer, but I read through since I touched the auth middleware last quarter. This is solid work. One thing I wanted to flag:

**rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py, line 129:**
The `process_response` hook sets the `Set-Cookie` header with the new token after rotation. The `SameSite` attribute is set to `Lax` — just confirming that's intentional? Our current Django session cookie uses `Lax` too, so it's consistent. But worth calling out since some enterprise customers have asked about `Strict` mode.

**rivulet/auth/session_middleware.py, line 134:**

**Item: Start time-boxing investigation/research spikes**
Written by: Angela Fong

[Angela Fong — 3:14 PM]
This sprint went well but I want to flag something for the future. The partner onboarding API required me to evaluate three different API key generation libraries. I spent a full day comparing them before choosing one. In hindsight, the differences were marginal and any of them would have worked. I should have time-boxed the research to 2 hours and just picked one.

[Simone Osei — 3:14 PM]
Which three were you looking at? I'm curious because I've done similar evaluations before and run into the same thing.

[Angela Fong — 3:15 PM]
`python-jose`, `PyJWT`, and `authlib`. They all support JWK generation, they're all maintained, they all have decent documentation. I went deep on benchmarking their key generation throughput, which was completely irrelevant — we generate maybe 50 keys a day. I was optimizing for a scale we'll never hit.

[Derek Lane — 3:15 PM]
This is a common trap in engineering — analysis paralysis on library/tool selection. I've seen teams spend a week evaluating message queues when any of the top 3 options would have been fine.

[Marcus Webb — 3:16 PM]
I've done the same thing. When we were picking the state machine library for the reconciliation work, I spent half a day comparing `transitions` vs `python-statemachine` vs rolling our own. Derek pulled me out of it — said "just pick transitions, it has the most GitHub stars and the docs are fine."

[Priti Sharma — 3:16 PM]
What about consequential choices? Some library decisions ARE important — like choosing between a maintained and an abandoned library.

[Angela Fong — 3:17 PM]
Agreed. I'm specifically talking about choices between equivalent options. When the top candidates are all well-maintained, popular, and fit the use case, stop comparing and pick one.

[Priti Sharma — 3:17 PM]
That distinction matters. We should be clear about it in whatever guidance we write. "Equivalent options" is the key phrase. If one library hasn't had a commit in two years, that's not an equivalent option.

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:18 PM]
I ran into this last month with the Grafana provisioning approach — Terraform vs Helm chart vs raw JSON. Spent four hours going back and forth. Derek eventually said "we already use Terraform for everything else, just use Terraform." Five-second decision if I'd just asked.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:18 PM]
Proposal: investigation/research spikes get a time-box in the ticket description. Default: 2 hours for library/tool selection, 4 hours for architectural research, 1 day for proof-of-concept spikes. If you hit the time-box without a conclusion, bring it to the team.

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:19 PM]
That's practical. The "bring it to the team" escape valve is important — sometimes research genuinely needs more time.

[Derek Lane — 3:19 PM]
And when you do bring it to the team, come with your top 2 options and the specific dimension you can't decide on. Don't come with "I don't know what to pick." Come with "I'm stuck between A and B, and the deciding factor is X. What do you all think about X?"

[Simone Osei — 3:20 PM]
Can we add the time-box defaults to the Jira ticket template so people see them when they create a ticket?

[Kai Nakamura — 3:20 PM]
Yes, I'll add it to the investigation/spike template. Good call.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:20 PM]
**Decision: We decided to time-box all investigation/research spikes.** Default time-boxes: 2 hours for library/tool selection, 4 hours for architectural research, 1 day for POC spikes. Exceeding the time-box requires team discussion.

**Item: Stop writing test descriptions in comments — use descriptive test names instead**
Written by: Yuto Hayashi

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:38 PM]
I was reviewing Marcus's reconciliation tests and found a lot of this pattern:

```python
def test_retry_case_1(self):
    """Test that a failed reconciliation with a transient error is retried
    up to 3 times with exponential backoff."""
    ...

def test_retry_case_2(self):
    """Test that a failed reconciliation with a permanent error is
    not retried and goes directly to the dead-letter queue."""
    ...
```

When the test fails in CI, you see `FAILED test_retry_case_1` — which tells you nothing. If the test was named `test_transient_error_retries_three_times_with_exponential_backoff`, the failure message is self-documenting.

[Marcus Webb — 3:39 PM]
Fair point. I was being lazy. The comment has the information but the test name doesn't.

[Angela Fong — 3:40 PM]
I had the same issue last sprint when a test failed in the partner API suite while I was on-call. The test was called `test_edge_case_3`. I had to open the file and read the docstring to understand what it was even testing. At 2am that's not fun.

[Derek Lane — 3:40 PM]
This is a code review issue. We should be catching this in reviews. But it's also a convention issue — if the convention is clear, people will follow it.

[Priti Sharma — 3:41 PM]
There's also the issue of test output readability. When you run pytest with verbose mode, you get a list of test names. If those names are descriptive, the test output reads like a specification of the system's behavior. `test_case_1, test_case_2, test_case_3` reads like nothing.

[Simone Osei — 3:41 PM]
Can we add a linting rule for minimum test name length? Something like "test function names must have at least 5 words separated by underscores."

[Angela Fong — 3:42 PM]
Five words is arbitrary. I'd rather focus on the principle: test names should describe the behavior being tested, not just "case 1, case 2."

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:42 PM]
The principle is what matters. But having a concrete example helps people follow it. Something like: instead of `test_validate_input`, write `test_validate_input_rejects_negative_amounts`. Instead of `test_retry_logic`, write `test_retry_exhausted_sends_to_dead_letter_queue`.

[Marcus Webb — 3:43 PM]
I'll go back and rename the reconciliation tests this sprint. Should only take an hour or so and it'll make the test suite much more readable.

[Derek Lane — 3:43 PM]
You don't need to do that as a separate task — just rename them when you're next touching those files. Boy Scout rule.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:43 PM]
**Decision: We decided to adopt a convention of descriptive test names that describe the tested behavior.** Format: `test_<condition>_<expected_behavior>`. Reviewers should flag generic test names (e.g., `test_case_1`) in code reviews. No automated linting for now — we'll revisit if compliance is low.

**Item: Stop skipping unit tests when "it's just a small change"**
Written by: Priti Sharma

[Priti Sharma — 3:45 PM]
The invoice rounding bug from last sprint was a "small change" — a one-line modification to the currency conversion function. No new test was written because "it's just a rounding fix, the existing tests cover it." Except the existing tests only covered USD→EUR, not EUR→USD. The asymmetric rounding was the whole bug.

[Marcus Webb — 3:46 PM]
Guilty. I made that change and skipped the test because I was in a hurry. Won't do it again.

[Simone Osei — 3:46 PM]
To be fair, the PR had 4 approvals and none of us caught it either. It's not just a Marcus problem — it's a team problem. We all looked at a one-line change and thought "that's trivial, it's fine."

[Derek Lane — 3:47 PM]
This should be a code review gate. No PR merged without tests for the changed behavior. Period.

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:47 PM]
What counts as "tests for the changed behavior" though? If I refactor a function and the existing tests still pass, do I need new tests?

[Derek Lane — 3:48 PM]
If the existing tests cover the behavior that changed, no. But the reviewer should verify that. The question isn't "are there tests" — it's "do the tests cover the thing that changed." In the rounding bug case, the answer was no. The existing tests covered one direction but not the other.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:48 PM]
We already have a coverage requirement (80% on changed files) but that's a blunt instrument — you can hit 80% coverage without testing the important behavior.

[Priti Sharma — 3:49 PM]
Exactly. Coverage is necessary but not sufficient. You can have 100% line coverage and still miss the rounding bug if your test only checks `assert result > 0` instead of `assert result == 142.37`.

[Angela Fong — 3:49 PM]
What about requiring that every PR description include a "Test Plan" section that describes what was tested and why? The reviewer checks the test plan against the code changes and flags gaps.

[Marcus Webb — 3:50 PM]
I like that. It forces the author to think about testing before they submit the PR, not after. And it gives the reviewer a checklist to verify against.

[Simone Osei — 3:50 PM]
Can we make it a required field in the PR template? So you literally can't submit a PR without filling it in?

[Kai Nakamura — 3:50 PM]
GitHub doesn't enforce it at that level — you can leave template sections empty. But we can make it a code review norm. If the Test Plan section is empty, the reviewer sends it back.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:50 PM]
**Decision: We decided to add a mandatory "Test Plan" section to our PR template.** Every PR must describe what was tested and how. Reviewers must verify the test plan covers the changed behavior. This is a code review gate — PRs without a test plan are not reviewed.

**Item: Continue design reviews for 5+ point tickets**
Written by: Derek Lane, Angela Fong, Simone Osei

[Derek Lane — 3:52 PM]
The design review policy from last sprint's retro was the single biggest improvement this sprint. The payment reconciliation design review caught 3 edge cases:
1. What happens when a retry succeeds but the original request also eventually succeeds? (duplicate processing)
2. What happens when the dead-letter queue is full? (backpressure)
3. What happens when the state machine receives an event for a state it's already transitioned out of? (stale event)

ITEM 1: CACHING STRATEGY PROPOSAL AND DECISION (10:00–10:48 AM)

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 10:00 AM]
Alright, let's get started. I know people have calls at 11:30 so we'll try to stay on time. David, you've got the floor for the caching proposal. I know a few of you already read through the doc he shared yesterday — if you haven't, don't worry, he's going to walk through the whole thing. David, go ahead.

[David Okafor — 10:01 AM]
Thanks Priya. OK so — I've been working on a caching strategy for our API layer and I want to walk through the proposal and get a decision today. We've been running without any structured caching and it's starting to hurt — the dashboard widgets are the most visible symptom but it's a broader problem.

Let me share the data. I instrumented our top 20 API endpoints last week with response time tracking. I added a middleware that captures P50, P95, and P99 latency for each endpoint, bucketed by tenant tier. The instrumentation PR is #1862 if anyone wants to look at the implementation — it's a lightweight decorator pattern, no performance impact on the endpoints themselves. Here's what I found:

**Most expensive endpoints (P95 response time):**
1. `GET /api/dashboards/{id}/widgets` — 2,800ms (multiple aggregation queries)
2. `GET /api/analytics/query` — 1,200ms (arbitrary query execution)
3. `GET /api/tenants/{id}/usage` — 890ms (billing aggregation)
4. `GET /api/data-sources` — 420ms (joins across 3 tables)
5. `GET /api/dashboards` — 380ms (list with summary stats)

For reference, the next five are all under 200ms and don't need attention right now.

The dashboard widgets endpoint is the worst offender. When a user opens a dashboard with 8 widgets, the frontend fires 8 parallel requests, each running an aggregation query. That's 8 x 2.8s = 22.4 seconds of database time per dashboard load. Users are complaining.

[Lin Zhang — 10:06 AM]
Just to add some frontend context — the 8 parallel requests happen because each widget type has its own data fetcher. We fire them all with `Promise.all()` on mount. The loading spinner shows until the slowest widget resolves, so even though most widgets finish in 1-2 seconds, the user sees the spinner until the last one at 2.8 seconds. It's a bad experience. We've had three support tickets about it in the last two weeks alone.

[David Okafor — 10:07 AM]
Right. And it's getting worse as we onboard more tenants with larger datasets. The events table is at 48 million rows now and growing by about 2 million a week.

But here's the thing: most of this data doesn't change frequently. Analytics data is append-only — once an event is recorded, it doesn't change. Dashboard configurations change maybe once a day. Usage stats are recomputed hourly. We're doing expensive queries for data that's essentially static between updates.

[Wendy Nakamura — Zoom — 10:08 AM]
David, quick question before you get into the proposal — did the instrumentation show any pattern by tenant? I'm wondering if this is concentrated on a few heavy tenants or distributed evenly.

[David Okafor — 10:09 AM]
Good question. It's somewhat concentrated. Our top 5 tenants by data volume account for about 60% of the total query time. But even smaller tenants are seeing 800ms+ on the widgets endpoint because the query plan is suboptimal regardless of data size — I'll cover that fix in agenda item 2. The caching benefits everyone, but the biggest impact will be on those high-volume tenants.

OK, so the proposal.

**Proposal: Redis caching layer with a 5-minute TTL for API responses.**

Here's the architecture:

```
Client → API Server → Cache Check (Redis) → Hit? → Return cached response
                                           → Miss? → Execute query → Store in Redis (5-min TTL) → Return response
```

**Cache key structure:**
```
cache:{endpoint}:{tenant_id}:{hash_of_query_params}
```

The tenant_id in the key ensures tenant isolation — Tenant A can never receive Tenant B's cached data. The hash is SHA-256 of the sorted query parameters, so `?start=2025-01-01&end=2025-06-01` and `?end=2025-06-01&start=2025-01-01` produce the same cache key.

[Tomas Eriksson — Zoom — 10:12 AM]
David, just to clarify — are you hashing the full query string or the normalized parameters? I've seen bugs where URL encoding differences cause cache misses for semantically identical requests.

[David Okafor — 10:12 AM]
Good point. I'm normalizing first — lowercasing keys, sorting alphabetically, then hashing the canonical form. So `?Start=2025-01-01` and `?start=2025-01-01` hit the same cache entry. Here's the normalization function:

```python
def normalize_cache_params(params: dict) -> str:
    """Produce a canonical string from query params for cache key hashing."""
    normalized = {k.lower(): str(v) for k, v in sorted(params.items())}
    return json.dumps(normalized, sort_keys=True, separators=(',', ':'))
```

[Tomas Eriksson — Zoom — 10:13 AM]
That works. Thanks.

[David Okafor — 10:13 AM]
OK continuing with the proposal.

**TTL strategy:**
- Dashboard widget data: 5 minutes (data is append-only, 5-min staleness is acceptable)
- Dashboard list/config: 60 seconds (config changes should reflect quickly)
- Analytics query results: 5 minutes (same as widgets)
- Usage/billing data: 15 minutes (recomputed hourly anyway)
- Data source list: 30 seconds (infrequently changes but should be responsive)

I've put together a table of all endpoints with their proposed TTLs in the Confluence doc. The five I listed are the ones that matter most. Everything else either has a TTL of 30 seconds or isn't worth caching because it's already fast.

[Anika Patel — 10:15 AM]
For the billing data — 15-minute TTL — do we need to worry about tenants seeing stale usage numbers on their settings page? If someone just upgraded their plan, would they see old usage stats?

[David Okafor — 10:15 AM]
The usage endpoint aggregates historical data. Plan changes are a different endpoint that isn't cached. So they'd see their new plan instantly but the usage chart might show data up to 15 minutes old, which is already the case because the usage aggregation job runs hourly. No practical difference for the user.

[Anika Patel — 10:16 AM]
Got it, that makes sense.

[David Okafor — 10:16 AM]
**Cache invalidation:**
- TTL-based expiry is the primary mechanism (simple, reliable)
- Explicit invalidation on write: when a dashboard is updated, we invalidate all cache keys for that dashboard
- Bulk invalidation: `cache:flush:{tenant_id}:*` pattern delete for tenant-level cache reset
- No cache warming — we'll rely on the first request after expiry to populate the cache (miss penalty is acceptable)

For the explicit invalidation, I'm using Redis `SCAN` with pattern matching rather than `KEYS` to avoid blocking Redis on large keyspaces. The invalidation happens asynchronously — the write endpoint returns immediately and fires a background task to clear cache entries.

```python
async def invalidate_dashboard_cache(tenant_id: str, dashboard_id: str):
    """Invalidate all cache entries for a specific dashboard."""
    pattern = f"cache:dashboards/{dashboard_id}/*:{tenant_id}:*"
    cursor = 0
    while True:
        cursor, keys = await cache_redis.scan(cursor, match=pattern, count=100)
        if keys:
            await cache_redis.delete(*keys)
        if cursor == 0:
            break
```

[Mike Torres — 10:18 AM]

[David Okafor — 10:19 AM]
I ran the numbers. Worst case: a tenant with 20 dashboards, each with 8 widgets, each queried with 5 different date ranges = 800 cache keys per tenant. With 200 tenants, that's 160K keys total. Each key's value is a serialized JSON response averaging 2-5KB. Total memory: roughly 400MB-800MB, well within the 13GB we're provisioning.

[Mike Torres — 10:20 AM]

[David Okafor — 10:20 AM]
Exactly.

I'm proposing we use a separate Redis instance from the session store. Sessions are critical infrastructure — if the session Redis has a memory issue because of cache data, we don't want it to affect authentication. Carlos, is that feasible?

I've added a circuit breaker around the Redis calls: if we get 5 consecutive connection failures within 30 seconds, we trip the circuit and bypass cache entirely for 60 seconds before retrying. Here's the config:

```python
CACHE_CIRCUIT_BREAKER = {
    "failure_threshold": 5,
    "recovery_timeout": 60,  # seconds
    "expected_exceptions": [ConnectionError, TimeoutError, RedisError],
}
```

[Sarah Kim — 10:28 AM]

Keeping them separate means we can configure each cluster's eviction policy independently.

[James Holden — 10:29 AM]

[David Okafor — 10:30 AM]
Based on the access patterns I measured:
- Dashboard widget endpoint: P95 drops from 2,800ms to ~50ms on cache hit (the first load after expiry is still 2,800ms)
- Cache hit rate estimate: 75-85% during working hours (users tend to view the same dashboards repeatedly)
- Database load reduction: 30-40% fewer queries during peak hours
- Connection pool headroom: significant — cached requests don't touch the database at all

For the hit rate estimate, I looked at our access logs for the last 30 days. The top 50 dashboard-tenant pairs account for 78% of all widget requests. These dashboards are typically viewed by multiple users throughout the day — team dashboards, executive dashboards, ops dashboards. With a 5-minute TTL, the first viewer after each expiry takes the hit, and everyone else gets cached responses.

Net effect: most dashboard loads will feel instant (sub-100ms) instead of taking 3+ seconds.

[Lin Zhang — 10:33 AM]

[David Okafor — 10:35 AM]

[Wendy Nakamura — Zoom — 10:35 AM]

[James Holden — 10:36 AM]
What about cache stampede? If a popular dashboard's cache expires and 50 users request it simultaneously, we'd get 50 cache misses all hitting the database at once.

[David Okafor — 10:36 AM]
Good catch. I'm implementing a cache-aside pattern with a distributed lock. When a cache miss occurs, the first request acquires a lock (Redis SETNX with a 5-second TTL), executes the query, and populates the cache. Subsequent requests during the lock period wait briefly (100ms) and then check the cache again. If the cache is populated, they get the cached response. If the lock times out without populating the cache, they fall through to the database.

```python
def get_cached_or_compute(cache_key, compute_fn, ttl):
    # Try cache first
    cached = redis.get(cache_key)
    if cached:
        return deserialize(cached)

    # Try to acquire computation lock
    lock_key = f"lock:{cache_key}"
    acquired = redis.set(lock_key, "1", nx=True, ex=5)

    if acquired:
        # We got the lock — compute and cache
        try:
            result = compute_fn()
            redis.set(cache_key, serialize(result), ex=ttl)
            return result
        finally:
            redis.delete(lock_key)
    else:
        # Someone else is computing — wait and check cache
        time.sleep(0.1)
        cached = redis.get(cache_key)
        if cached:
            return deserialize(cached)
        # Lock holder might have failed — compute directly
        return compute_fn()
```

The lock TTL of 5 seconds is a safety net. If the lock holder crashes or takes too long, the lock auto-expires and the next requester can take over. We never get stuck in a permanent lock state.

[Mike Torres — 10:40 AM]
That's the right pattern. The sleep(0.1) is a bit crude though — could we use Redis pub/sub to notify waiters when the cache is populated?

[David Okafor — 10:40 AM]
We could, but it adds complexity for marginal benefit. The 100ms wait covers 95% of cases (our slowest query is 2.8s, and even the median dashboard query completes in ~500ms, so 100ms is usually enough for the lock holder to finish). ITEM 2: DASHBOARD WIDGET PERFORMANCE UPDATE (10:48–11:05 AM)

[David Okafor — 10:48 AM]
Quick update on the widget performance ticket (FALCON-09). Two separate improvements:

First, the composite index fix I mentioned in standup. I added a composite index on `(tenant_id, event_type, created_at)` to the events table. The aggregation query for widgets was doing a full scan on 48M rows because the existing index only covered `(tenant_id, created_at)` and the query filters on `event_type` too. After adding the index:

Before:
```
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Seq Scan on events → 2,340ms, rows=48,000,000
```

After:
```
EXPLAIN ANALYZE: Index Scan using idx_events_tenant_type_created → 89ms, rows=12,400
```

That's a 26x improvement on the database side. Combined with the caching layer, dashboard widgets should go from 2.8 seconds to sub-100ms for cached responses and ~150ms for cache misses.

[Tomas Eriksson — Zoom — 10:51 AM]
David, did adding that index have any impact on write performance? We're doing a lot of INSERTs into the events table.

[David Okafor — 10:52 AM]
Minimal. I checked the INSERT latency before and after: P95 went from 1.2ms to 1.4ms. The index is on three columns but they're all narrow types — UUID, varchar(50), and timestamp. The B-tree maintenance cost is negligible compared to the query improvement. I ran the migration during our low-traffic window on Wednesday night and the index creation took about 3 minutes on the 48M row table using `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY` so there was no table lock.

[Sarah Kim — 10:53 AM]
Good call using `CONCURRENTLY`. I've been burned by index creation locks before.

[David Okafor — 10:53 AM]
Second, I refactored the widget query builder to push aggregation down to the database instead of fetching raw rows and aggregating in Python. The old code was doing:

```python
# Old: fetch all events, aggregate in Python
events = Event.objects.filter(tenant=tenant, type=event_type, created__range=(start, end))
result = {}
for event in events:
    key = event.created.date()
    result.setdefault(key, []).append(event.value)
# ... compute averages, sums, etc.
```

New code:
```python
# New: aggregate in PostgreSQL
result = (
    Event.objects
    .filter(tenant=tenant, type=event_type, created__range=(start, end))
    .values(day=TruncDate('created'))
    .annotate(avg_value=Avg('value'), sum_value=Sum('value'), count=Count('id'))
    .order_by('day')
)
```

This reduces data transfer from database to application by ~99% for large date ranges. Instead of fetching 50K rows and aggregating in Python, we get back 30 pre-aggregated rows.

[Mike Torres — 10:56 AM]
That's a classic optimization. I'm surprised it wasn't done that way originally.

[David Okafor — 10:56 AM]
Legacy code. The original implementation was written when the events table had 10K rows and the Python aggregation was fast enough. Nobody revisited it as the table grew. This is one of those gradual degradation issues — it worked fine for a year and then suddenly it didn't.

[Wendy Nakamura — Zoom — 10:57 AM]
Did the aggregation refactor change any of the returned values? I want to make sure our integration tests are covering the new query path.

[David Okafor — 10:57 AM]
The output format is identical — same JSON shape, same field names. The values are mathematically equivalent, though there might be tiny floating-point differences in averages because PostgreSQL and Python handle float division slightly differently. I added a tolerance check in the tests.

[Sarah Kim — 10:58 AM]
That's a huge improvement. Were there any test failures from the refactor?

[David Okafor — 10:58 AM]
Two tests failed because they were testing the Python aggregation logic directly. I updated them to test the query output instead. All tests pass now. PR #1865, merged yesterday. I also added a new test that compares the old aggregation path with the new one on a fixture dataset to verify equivalence, just as a safety net.

[Priya Chandrasekaran — 10:59 AM]
Great work. This was a top customer complaint. What's the measured impact in staging?

[David Okafor — 11:00 AM]
Staging numbers with the new index and the push-down aggregation (no cache yet):
- Dashboard with 8 widgets: 1.2 seconds total (down from 8+ seconds)
- Single widget load: 150ms P95 (down from 2,800ms)

Once the cache layer is in place, repeat loads will be sub-50ms.

[Wendy Nakamura — Zoom — 11:01 AM]
I ran the regression suite against staging after the merge and everything passed. I also did a manual walkthrough on the Analytics Pro dashboard, which has the most widgets — it's noticeably faster. Page load felt almost instant compared to last week.

Thanks Wendy. Glad to hear it's holding up in staging.

TOPIC 1: ON-CALL ROTATION AND BURNOUT (3:14 PM — 3:32 PM)

[Marcus Webb — 3:14 PM]
I wrote this topic. We have 7 engineers on the on-call rotation. That means each person is on-call every 7 weeks. The problem is that on-call weeks are exhausting — you're checking alerts all evening and weekend, and you still need to do sprint work during the day. After on-call, I need a recovery day but I don't take one because there's no policy for it.

I'm not complaining — I understand on-call is part of the role. But there's no structural support for recovery, and that's going to cause problems long-term.

[Simone Osei — 3:16 PM]
I feel the same way. My last on-call week had the partner API key rotation incident on a Wednesday night and two false alarms over the weekend. I was sleep-deprived by Monday and my productivity was visibly lower all Sprint 16 Day 1.

The Sunday false alarm was at 3:47 AM — high memory utilization on the staging environment. Staging. Not even production. I was awake for an hour before I realized it was a non-issue.

[Angela Fong — 3:16 PM]
Wait, staging alerts page on-call? That shouldn't be happening.

[Simone Osei — 3:17 PM]
It does. The alert rules don't distinguish between environments for some of the infrastructure monitors.

[Derek Lane — 3:17 PM]
The alert noise is a factor too. We're averaging 8 alerts per on-call week. Of those, about 3 are false positives. That means the on-call person is getting woken up or interrupted for things that aren't real problems.

I pulled the PagerDuty stats for the last 4 on-call rotations. Sprint 13: 9 alerts, 4 false. Sprint 14: 7 alerts, 2 false. Sprint 15: 6 alerts, 3 false. Sprint 16: 8 alerts, 3 false. So we're averaging about 7-8 total, 3 false.

[Yuto Hayashi — 3:18 PM]
At my previous company, we had a "comp day" policy — after an on-call week with overnight pages, you got a day off the following Monday. It made a huge difference for morale and prevented burnout-driven attrition.

They framed it not as a perk but as an operational necessity. Tired engineers make worse decisions and write buggier code. The comp day pays for itself.

[Angela Fong — 3:19 PM]
I'd support that. But we also need to reduce the alert noise. If we're getting 3 false positives per week, we need to tune our alerting thresholds. That's just noise fatigue.

[Priti Sharma — 3:20 PM]
Agreed on both. The false positive rate is nearly 40%. That's way too high. Every false alarm trains people to take alerts less seriously. That's how you miss a real incident.

[Kai Nakamura — 3:20 PM]
Two problems here: on-call recovery and alert quality. Let's address both.

For on-call recovery: proposal — after any on-call week with overnight pages (10pm-7am), the engineer gets the following Monday as a comp day. It's not PTO — it's part of the on-call cost of doing business.

For alert quality: proposal — dedicate 2 story points per sprint to alert tuning. The engineer coming off on-call reviews their week's alerts and tunes the ones that were false positives. This turns on-call pain into systematic improvement.

[Marcus Webb — 3:21 PM]
I like that framing — "turns on-call pain into systematic improvement." The person who just experienced the false alarms is the best person to tune them because the frustration is fresh and the context is loaded.

- End-to-end (e2e) tests: 86

Total: 745 tests.

The test pyramid says we should have many unit tests, fewer integration tests, and fewest e2e tests. We have it backwards — more integration tests than unit tests.

Why this matters: our CI pipeline takes 4 minutes 50 seconds. Of that, unit tests take 30 seconds, integration tests take 2 minutes 40 seconds, and e2e tests take 1 minute 40 seconds. The integration tests are the bottleneck.

More importantly, integration tests are flaky. Of the 14 flaky tests we fixed this sprint, 12 were integration tests. They depend on database state, network calls, and timing — all sources of non-determinism.

[Derek Lane — 3:36 PM]

[Angela Fong — 3:37 PM]
