ITEM 1: API VERSIONING STRATEGY (3:00–3:42 PM)

[Sarah Kim — 3:00 PM]
Thanks everyone. So this has been on the backlog for a while and the multi-workspace work is going to force our hand. When we add workspace_id to API endpoints, we're making a breaking change to the API contract. We need a versioning strategy before we can ship multi-workspace to customers.

Let me walk through the options and then propose a direction. I put a lot of thought into this over the weekend so bear with me — there's a lot of ground to cover.

**Option 1: URL path versioning**
```
GET /api/v1/dashboards
GET /api/v2/dashboards?workspace_id=ws_abc123
```
Pros: Explicit, easy to route, easy to document, clients know exactly which version they're hitting. Every request wears its version on its sleeve. You can see it in access logs, in curl commands, in browser dev tools. No ambiguity.
Cons: URL proliferation, harder to share links (which version?), some REST purists consider it an anti-pattern because the version isn't part of the resource's identity.

**Option 2: Header-based versioning**
```
GET /api/dashboards
Accept: application/vnd.rivulet.v2+json
```
Pros: Clean URLs, RESTful, version is metadata not resource identity. GitHub's API actually supports this pattern as an alternative to their path versioning.
Cons: Harder to test (need to set headers), harder to document, CDN caching complications, some clients don't easily support custom Accept headers. I tested this with Postman and it's fine, but a lot of enterprise customers are using simple curl scripts or low-code tools like Zapier that don't have easy header customization.

**Option 3: Query parameter versioning**
```
GET /api/dashboards?api_version=2
```
Pros: Easy to test, explicit, works with any client.
Cons: Pollutes the query string, easy to forget, mixed with business parameters. Also creates weird interactions with our existing pagination and filter params — you'd see `?api_version=2&page=3&sort=created_at` and it's messy.

[Tom Nguyen — 3:08 PM]
Quick question — did you look at date-based versioning? I've seen some APIs use dates like `2025-06-15` instead of v1/v2.

[Sarah Kim — 3:09 PM]
I did look at that. Stripe actually uses date-based versioning for their changelog — like `Stripe-Version: 2023-10-16`. The problem is it creates an explosion of versions. Every breaking change is a new version date, and you end up maintaining dozens of effective versions. It works for Stripe because they have a dedicated API compatibility team. We don't have that luxury. I think major version numbers are more manageable for a team our size.

[Tom Nguyen — 3:10 PM]
Fair enough. Thanks for looking into it.

[Sarah Kim — 3:10 PM]
My recommendation: **Option 1, URL path versioning.** Here's why:

1. Our customers are enterprise B2B. They build integrations against our API. Explicit versioning in the URL makes it impossible to accidentally hit the wrong version. When they're debugging, the version is right there in the URL.

2. We're building a customer-facing API — not an internal microservice. Discoverability matters more than REST purity.

3. Stripe, GitHub, and Twilio all use URL path versioning. Our customers are used to it.

4. It simplifies our routing layer. We can route `/api/v1/*` and `/api/v2/*` to different handler modules without any middleware magic.

[Elena Marchetti — 3:13 PM]
From the docs side, I strongly prefer path versioning. It makes the documentation structure much cleaner — we can have separate doc sections for v1 and v2, and customers can see exactly which version they're reading about in the URL. With header-based versioning, documenting the differences becomes really confusing.

[Sarah Kim — 3:14 PM]
Great point, Elena. That's another practical advantage I should have included.

**Implementation plan:**

Phase 1: Add v1 prefix to all existing endpoints. Deploy as a transparent proxy — `/api/v1/dashboards` routes to the same handler as `/api/dashboards`. The old unprefixed URLs continue to work but return a `Deprecation` header.

I'm thinking the deprecation header would look like this:
```
Deprecation: true
Sunset: 2026-06-15
Link: <https://docs.rivulet.io/api/migration/v1>; rel="deprecation"
```
That follows RFC 8594 for the Sunset header and RFC 8288 for the Link header. Gives clients machine-readable migration info.

Phase 2: Build v2 endpoints for multi-workspace. These require workspace_id and have updated response schemas. v1 endpoints continue to work for tenants that haven't adopted workspaces (they hit the "default" workspace implicitly).

For Phase 2, the v2 response schema changes look like this for the dashboards endpoint:
```json
// v1 response
{
  "id": "dash_abc123",
  "name": "Revenue Overview",
  "widgets": [...]
}

// v2 response
{
  "id": "dash_abc123",
  "name": "Revenue Overview",
  "workspace_id": "ws_xyz789",
  "workspace_name": "Finance Team",
  "widgets": [...]
}
```

Phase 3 (6 months out): Deprecation notice for v1. Give customers 6 months to migrate. Remove v1 after 12 months.

**Versioning policy (proposed):**
- New major version only for breaking changes (removed fields, changed types, new required parameters)
- Additive changes (new optional fields, new endpoints) are added to the current version
- We maintain at most 2 major versions simultaneously
- Each version has a minimum 12-month support window after deprecation notice

[Mike Torres — 3:15 PM]

Separate URL configs. `urls_v1.py` and `urls_v2.py`, each included under their prefix in the main `urls.py`. This gives us clean separation — v1 and v2 handlers can live in different modules without any routing magic.

```python
# urls.py
urlpatterns = [
    path('api/v1/', include('rivulet.api.v1.urls')),
    path('api/v2/', include('rivulet.api.v2.urls')),
    # Legacy unprefixed routes (deprecated)
    path('api/', include('rivulet.api.v1.urls')),
]
```

For shared logic, the view code lives in a common module and the version-specific URL configs point to thin wrapper views that handle schema differences.

[Sarah Kim — 3:23 PM]
I was thinking WebSocket versioning would be protocol-level — the client specifies the protocol version in the WS handshake:
```
Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: rivulet-ws-v1
```
The server negotiates the protocol version and sends messages in the appropriate format. This is how most WS APIs handle versioning.

For reference, the connection flow would look like:
```
Client → ws://api.rivulet.io/ws
Headers:
  Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: rivulet-ws-v1
  Authorization: Bearer <token>

Server → 101 Switching Protocols
  Sec-WebSocket-Protocol: rivulet-ws-v1
```

If the client requests a version we don't support, we reject the handshake with a 400 and an error body listing supported versions.

ITEM 2: MULTI-WORKSPACE DATABASE MIGRATION PLAN (3:42–4:02 PM)

[David Okafor — 3:42 PM]
I've drafted the database migration plan for multi-workspace. Let me walk through the approach.

The goal: add `workspace_id` to all tenant-scoped tables, with data isolation guarantees.

**Tables affected** (in migration order):
1. `workspaces` — new table: (id, tenant_id, name, created_at, updated_at, is_default)
2. `dashboards` — add workspace_id FK
3. `data_sources` — add workspace_id FK
4. `events` — add workspace_id FK (the big one: 48M rows)
5. `export_jobs` — add workspace_id FK
6. `permissions` — restructure to include workspace_id scope
7. `notifications` — add workspace_id FK

[Tom Nguyen — 3:45 PM]
Forty-eight million rows — how big is the events table on disk right now?

[David Okafor — 3:45 PM]
About 14GB. The UUID column add is metadata-only in PostgreSQL so it won't rewrite the table. The backfill writes are the expensive part, but they're batched. I'll get to the numbers in a minute.

[Elena Marchetti — 3:46 PM]
Quick question — the `permissions` table restructure, is that also an additive change or is it more involved?

[David Okafor — 3:46 PM]
More involved. Currently permissions are tenant-scoped: a user has a role within a tenant. With workspaces, we need workspace-scoped permissions: a user has a role within a workspace within a tenant. I'm adding a `workspace_id` column to the permissions table, and the application code will check workspace-level permissions. Existing tenant-level permissions become "all workspaces" permissions via a default workspace assignment. I'll cover the details in a separate design doc — today I want to focus on the events table since that's the riskiest part.

**Migration strategy for the events table:**

Step 1: Create the `workspaces` table and auto-create a "Default" workspace for each existing tenant.

The migration for this step looks like:
```sql
CREATE TABLE workspaces (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    tenant_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES tenants(id),
    name VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT now(),
    is_default BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT false,
    UNIQUE(tenant_id, name)
);

CREATE INDEX idx_workspaces_tenant ON workspaces(tenant_id);

-- Auto-create default workspace for each existing tenant
INSERT INTO workspaces (tenant_id, name, is_default)
SELECT id, 'Default', true FROM tenants;
```

Step 2: Add `workspace_id` as a NULLABLE column to `events`. This is a metadata-only change in PostgreSQL — no table rewrite, instant.
```sql
ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN workspace_id UUID;
```

Step 3: Backfill `workspace_id` with the default workspace ID for each tenant. Batched UPDATE, 10K rows per batch, throttled to avoid lock contention.
```sql
UPDATE events SET workspace_id = (
    SELECT id FROM workspaces WHERE tenant_id = events.tenant_id AND is_default = true
)
WHERE workspace_id IS NULL
LIMIT 10000;
```
Estimated time: 48M rows ÷ 10K per batch × 50ms per batch = ~4 minutes. (PostgreSQL batch updates are fast when the source is indexed.)

[Mike Torres — 3:50 PM]
Are you running the backfill in a Django management command or as raw SQL?

[David Okafor — 3:50 PM]
Management command. That way we get logging, progress output, and we can pause/resume if needed. I'll add a `--batch-size` flag and a `--sleep-between-batches` flag for throttling. Something like:

```python
# management/commands/backfill_workspace_id.py
class Command(BaseCommand):
    def add_arguments(self, parser):
        parser.add_argument('--batch-size', type=int, default=10000)
        parser.add_argument('--sleep-ms', type=int, default=0)
        parser.add_argument('--dry-run', action='store_true')

    def handle(self, **options):
        batch_size = options['batch_size']
        total_updated = 0
        while True:
            with connection.cursor() as cursor:
                cursor.execute("""
                    UPDATE events SET workspace_id = (
                        SELECT id FROM workspaces
                        WHERE tenant_id = events.tenant_id AND is_default = true
                    )
                    WHERE id IN (
                        SELECT id FROM events
                        WHERE workspace_id IS NULL
                        LIMIT %s
                    )
                """, [batch_size])
                updated = cursor.rowcount
            total_updated += updated
            self.stdout.write(f"Updated {total_updated} rows...")
            if updated < batch_size:
                break
            if options['sleep_ms']:
                time.sleep(options['sleep_ms'] / 1000.0)
```

Step 4: Add NOT NULL constraint with NOT VALID option (doesn't scan the table):
```sql
ALTER TABLE events ADD CONSTRAINT events_workspace_id_not_null CHECK (workspace_id IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID;
```

Step 5: Validate the constraint in the background:
```sql
ALTER TABLE events VALIDATE CONSTRAINT events_workspace_id_not_null;
```
This does a full table scan but with a weak lock (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE) that doesn't block normal operations.

Step 6: Create the composite index concurrently:
```sql
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_events_workspace_type_created ON events (workspace_id, event_type, created_at);
```

[Tom Nguyen — 3:54 PM]
How long does the concurrent index creation take on 48M rows?

[David Okafor — 3:54 PM]
Tested on a staging copy — about 8 minutes for the composite index. It runs fully in the background, doesn't block reads or writes. The only thing it blocks is other DDL on the events table, which shouldn't be a problem since we're not running other migrations simultaneously.

**Zero-downtime guarantee:** Every step uses non-blocking DDL. The application continues serving requests throughout. The only behavioral change is that new events start including workspace_id after the code deploy, and the backfill handles existing data.

[Mike Torres — 3:52 PM]
What happens during the backfill if a new event is inserted without a workspace_id? The NOT NULL constraint isn't enforced yet.

[David Okafor — 3:53 PM]
Good catch. The application code needs to be updated to include workspace_id BEFORE we add the NOT NULL constraint. So the deploy order is:
1. Deploy migration (add nullable column)
2. Deploy application code (include workspace_id in all new events)
3. Run backfill for historical data
4. Add NOT NULL constraint
5. Validate constraint

Steps 2 and 3 can happen in any order since the constraint isn't enforced yet. But step 2 must happen before step 4.

[James Holden — 3:54 PM]

MEETING NOTES

[Omar Zafar — 2:02 PM]
Alright everyone, thanks for making time on a Friday. I know there's a happy hour at 4, so let's be efficient. This is the database selection discussion for InventoryHub v2. The legacy system uses Oracle 12c and we are NOT bringing Oracle into the new architecture. That's already decided. Legal is done with that licensing contract as of end of year and we are not renewing. The question is: what replaces it?

Let me frame the requirements for anyone who hasn't seen the requirements doc. InventoryHub v2 is our core inventory management service. It handles:
- Product catalog: 2.4 million SKUs across 340 warehouses
- Real-time stock levels: updated on every inbound/outbound transaction, ~18,000 transactions per minute at peak
- Reservation system: holds stock for orders in progress (must be ACID)
- Reporting: daily/weekly/monthly inventory reports, ad-hoc queries from the analytics team
- Multi-warehouse transfers: moving stock between locations with audit trail

Just to give everyone the full picture — the data model is relational. Products belong to categories, stock levels are per-warehouse, reservations reference both products and orders. Lots of foreign keys and constraints. This isn't a flexible-schema situation. The entities and their relationships are well-understood.

Non-functional requirements, and I want to be precise here because these will drive the evaluation:
- Write throughput: 18K writes/minute sustained, 30K burst
- Read throughput: 45K reads/minute sustained
- Latency: P99 < 50ms for stock level lookups
- Availability: 99.95% (we can tolerate ~22 minutes of downtime per month)
- Durability: zero data loss — inventory discrepancies cost real money
- Data volume: 500GB current, growing ~20% annually

Greg, from the QA side, anything you'd add to the requirements?

[Leah Kim — 2:08 PM]
Sure. My recommendation is **PostgreSQL 16**.

I sent out a detailed write-up last night, but I'll summarize the key points here.

Rationale:

1. **Relational model fit.** The inventory data model is inherently relational — products, categories, warehouses, stock levels, reservations, transfers. PostgreSQL's relational model maps directly to the domain. No impedance mismatch. We're not trying to squeeze a document model into relational tables or vice versa. The entities and relationships are clean.

2. **ACID compliance.** Reservations require strong consistency. When a customer reserves 5 units, that stock must be immediately unavailable to other orders. PostgreSQL's transaction isolation (Serializable or Repeatable Read) guarantees this without application-level locking. We can also use advisory locks if we need finer-grained control, but for the reservation use case, standard row-level locking via SELECT FOR UPDATE is sufficient and well-understood.

3. **Write performance.** PostgreSQL 16 can sustain 50K TPS on commodity hardware with proper tuning (pg_tune, shared_buffers, WAL configuration). Our requirement is 300 TPS (18K/min). We're at 0.6% of PostgreSQL's capacity. Plenty of headroom. I ran pgbench with a custom workload that simulates our reservation flow — on a db.r6g.xlarge (which is what Fatima would provision), I got 4,200 TPS sustained. That's 14x our peak requirement.

4. **Operational maturity.** We already run PostgreSQL 15 in production for three other services — AuthService, NotificationHub, and the billing ledger. The team has deep expertise. Fatima has the monitoring, backup, and failover infrastructure already built. We're not starting from zero.

5. **Ecosystem.** pg_cron for scheduled reporting queries, pg_partman for table partitioning (we'll want to partition stock_level_history by month), pgvector if we ever do similarity search on product descriptions. There's also excellent tooling — pgAdmin, pg_stat_statements for query analysis, pg_repack for online table reorganization.

6. **Cost.** Open source. Our existing RDS infrastructure supports it. No new licensing costs. And Greg, to your point — the official Docker image is 400MB, starts in 2 seconds, and our CI already has it.

The one concern I'll flag proactively: PostgreSQL's MVCC (Multi-Version Concurrency Control) can lead to table bloat on high-write tables. The stock_levels table will get 18K updates/minute. We'd need autovacuum aggressively tuned, and potentially partition the table by warehouse to limit vacuum scope. I've already drafted the partitioning strategy.

```sql
-- Proposed stock_levels schema
CREATE TABLE stock_levels (
    warehouse_id UUID NOT NULL,
    product_id UUID NOT NULL,
    available_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    reserved_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    in_transit_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    last_updated TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    version INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,  -- optimistic locking
    PRIMARY KEY (warehouse_id, product_id)
) PARTITION BY HASH (warehouse_id);

-- 16 partitions (one per hash bucket, roughly 21 warehouses each)
CREATE TABLE stock_levels_p0 PARTITION OF stock_levels FOR VALUES WITH (MODULUS 16, REMAINDER 0);
CREATE TABLE stock_levels_p1 PARTITION OF stock_levels FOR VALUES WITH (MODULUS 16, REMAINDER 1);
-- ... etc
```

The hash partitioning distributes writes evenly across partitions, which means autovacuum can process each partition independently. This prevents the "vacuum can't keep up" problem that plagues large un-partitioned tables. I've seen this exact approach work at my previous company for a table doing 25K updates/minute — vacuum never fell behind once we moved from a single table to 16 hash partitions.

[Omar Zafar — 2:16 PM]
Quick clarification — why hash on warehouse_id rather than product_id?

[Leah Kim — 2:16 PM]
Two reasons. First, most of our queries are warehouse-scoped — "show me all stock for warehouse X." Hash on warehouse_id means those queries hit a single partition. Second, warehouse_id has better cardinality distribution for our workload. The top 20 warehouses generate 60% of the transactions, so we get natural load balancing across partitions. If we hashed on product_id, the long-tail products would create uneven partition sizes.

[Omar Zafar — 2:17 PM]

[Ben Torres — 2:18 PM]
I also evaluated PostgreSQL — and I agree with everything Leah said about it. It's the safe, proven choice. But I want to present an alternative that I think deserves serious consideration: **CockroachDB**.

CockroachDB gives us everything PostgreSQL gives us (SQL, ACID, relational model) plus:

1. **Distributed by default.** CockroachDB is a distributed SQL database. Data is automatically replicated across 3+ nodes. If a node fails, the cluster continues serving reads and writes. No failover configuration, no replica promotion — it's built in. With PostgreSQL on Aurora, we have a primary and read replicas. If the primary fails, Aurora promotes a replica — that's a 30–60 second failover window. CockroachDB's consensus-based approach has zero failover time because there's no single primary.

2. **Horizontal write scaling.** With PostgreSQL, writes go to the primary. To scale writes, you scale up (bigger instance). With CockroachDB, writes are distributed across nodes. To scale, you add nodes. Our 18K writes/minute today might be 180K in 3 years — CockroachDB scales linearly. PostgreSQL scaling would mean progressively larger and more expensive instances.

3. **Multi-region capability.** If we expand to international warehouses (the 2026 roadmap mentions EMEA expansion), CockroachDB can replicate data to EU nodes with follower reads for low-latency access. PostgreSQL would need a complex replication setup. CockroachDB's locality-aware routing means EU queries read from EU nodes automatically.

4. **Built-in change data capture (CDC).** CockroachDB's changefeed feature streams row-level changes to Kafka. This is perfect for the analytics pipeline — Nadia can consume stock level changes in real-time without polling. You set up a changefeed with a single SQL statement and it just works.

The trade-offs, and I want to be upfront about these:
- **Cost.** CockroachDB Dedicated (managed cloud) is ~3x the cost of RDS PostgreSQL for equivalent specs. I got a rough quote from their sales team: $5,400/month for a 3-node cluster with our specs versus $1,840 for the Aurora setup Fatima specced.
- **Latency.** CockroachDB's distributed consensus (Raft) adds ~5ms to each write compared to PostgreSQL. Our P99 requirement is 50ms for reads, so this is fine for writes, but reads through distributed consensus are slower than PostgreSQL's local reads.
- **Operational unfamiliarity.** Nobody on the team has run CockroachDB in production. The debugging story is different — different monitoring tools, different query plan analysis, different operational runbooks.

Here are benchmark numbers I ran on a 3-node CockroachDB cluster (8 vCPUs, 32GB RAM per node). I used a workload modeled on our reservation flow — mixed reads and writes with the same cardinality as our production data:

| Operation | PostgreSQL 16 (single) | CockroachDB (3-node) |
|-----------|------------------------|----------------------|
| INSERT (stock update) | P50: 1.2ms, P99: 4.1ms | P50: 6.8ms, P99: 18ms |
| SELECT (stock lookup) | P50: 0.4ms, P99: 1.8ms | P50: 2.1ms, P99: 8.4ms |
| Transaction (reserve + update) | P50: 3.1ms, P99: 12ms | P50: 14ms, P99: 38ms |

PostgreSQL is faster in every category because it's a single-node system — no network round-trips for consensus. But CockroachDB is well within our latency requirements. The P99 for the full reservation transaction is 38ms on CockroachDB, and our budget is 50ms. That's a 12ms margin. Not huge, but sufficient.

SCHEMA DESIGN DISCUSSION (2:55 PM — 3:25 PM)

[Leah Kim — 2:55 PM]
Alright, for those of you still here. While we're all together, let me walk through the proposed schema. I want to validate the data model before I start coding the migration scripts this weekend.

I'll go table by table. Stop me if anything looks wrong or if I'm missing a use case.

**Core tables:**

```sql
-- Product catalog (2.4M rows, read-heavy)
CREATE TABLE products (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    sku VARCHAR(50) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    category_id UUID REFERENCES categories(id),
    unit_of_measure VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL,
    weight_grams INT,
    dimensions_cm JSONB,  -- {length, width, height}
    is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW()
);

-- Warehouse locations (340 rows, rarely changes)
CREATE TABLE warehouses (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    code VARCHAR(10) UNIQUE NOT NULL,
    name TEXT NOT NULL,
    region VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL,
    address JSONB NOT NULL,
    capacity_cubic_meters INT,
    is_active BOOLEAN DEFAULT true
);

-- Stock levels (2.4M × 340 = ~816M potential rows, but sparse — avg 12 warehouses per product)
-- Actual rows: ~28.8M
-- Write-heavy: 18K updates/minute
CREATE TABLE stock_levels (
    warehouse_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES warehouses(id),
    product_id UUID NOT NULL REFERENCES products(id),
    available_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (available_qty >= 0),
    reserved_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (reserved_qty >= 0),
    in_transit_qty INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 CHECK (in_transit_qty >= 0),
    last_transaction_id UUID,
    updated_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    version INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 1,
    PRIMARY KEY (warehouse_id, product_id)
) PARTITION BY HASH (warehouse_id);

-- Reservations (ACID-critical, moderate write rate)
CREATE TABLE reservations (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    order_id UUID NOT NULL,
    warehouse_id UUID NOT NULL,
    product_id UUID NOT NULL,
    quantity INT NOT NULL CHECK (quantity > 0),
    status VARCHAR(20) NOT NULL DEFAULT 'active',  -- active, fulfilled, cancelled, expired
    expires_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ DEFAULT NOW(),
    FOREIGN KEY (warehouse_id, product_id) REFERENCES stock_levels(warehouse_id, product_id)
);

-- Stock transactions (append-only audit trail)
CREATE TABLE stock_transactions (
    id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
    warehouse_id UUID NOT NULL,
    product_id UUID NOT NULL,
    transaction_type VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,  -- inbound, outbound, transfer_in, transfer_out, adjustment, reservation, release
    quantity INT NOT NULL,
    reference_id UUID,  -- order_id, transfer_id, or adjustment_id
    created_at TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL DEFAULT NOW(),
    created_by UUID NOT NULL
) PARTITION BY RANGE (created_at);
```

[Jake Petrov — 3:05 PM]
The reservation flow — walk me through the transaction. Customer places an order, we need to reserve stock. What's the SQL?

[Leah Kim — 3:06 PM]
Sure. Here's the full reservation flow as a single transaction:

```sql
BEGIN;

-- Lock the stock row (SELECT FOR UPDATE prevents concurrent modifications)
SELECT available_qty, version
FROM stock_levels
WHERE warehouse_id = $1 AND product_id = $2
FOR UPDATE;

-- Check availability (application logic)
-- If available_qty < requested_qty: ROLLBACK

-- Reserve stock
UPDATE stock_levels
SET available_qty = available_qty - $3,
    reserved_qty = reserved_qty + $3,
    version = version + 1,
    updated_at = NOW()
WHERE warehouse_id = $1 AND product_id = $2 AND version = $4;

-- Create reservation record
INSERT INTO reservations (order_id, warehouse_id, product_id, quantity, expires_at)
VALUES ($5, $1, $2, $3, NOW() + INTERVAL '30 minutes');

-- Create audit transaction
INSERT INTO stock_transactions (warehouse_id, product_id, transaction_type, quantity, reference_id, created_by)
VALUES ($1, $2, 'reservation', $3, $5, $6);

COMMIT;
```

The `SELECT FOR UPDATE` locks the specific stock row, preventing other transactions from modifying it until we commit. The `version` check provides optimistic locking as a safety net — if the version doesn't match, someone else modified the row between our SELECT and UPDATE (shouldn't happen with `FOR UPDATE`, but defense in depth). If the UPDATE affects 0 rows because the version changed, the application retries the whole transaction.

[Jake Petrov — 3:09 PM]
What's the lock scope? Does the `FOR UPDATE` lock only the one row, or the whole partition?

[Leah Kim — 3:09 PM]
Just the one row. PostgreSQL's row-level locking is granular — `FOR UPDATE` acquires a row-level exclusive lock on the matching rows only. Other transactions can read and write other rows in the same partition concurrently. The only contention is when two transactions try to reserve the same product in the same warehouse simultaneously, which is the exact case where we need serialization.

[Ben Torres — 3:10 PM]
What about reservation expiry? If a customer doesn't complete their order in 30 minutes, the reserved stock needs to be released. We can't have phantom reservations eating up available inventory.

[Leah Kim — 3:11 PM]
Two approaches. Option A: background job that runs every minute, finds expired reservations, releases stock. Option B: use pg_cron to run a stored procedure.

I prefer Option A because the business logic (release stock, update reservation status, create audit transaction) belongs in the application layer, not in a database procedure. Stored procedures are harder to test, harder to version control, and harder to debug in production. The job is simple and runs in the application's existing task scheduler:

```python
async def release_expired_reservations():
    expired = await Reservation.filter(
        status='active',
        expires_at__lt=now()
    ).select_for_update()

    for reservation in expired:
        async with transaction.atomic():
            await StockLevel.filter(
                warehouse_id=reservation.warehouse_id,
                product_id=reservation.product_id
            ).update(
                available_qty=F('available_qty') + reservation.quantity,
                reserved_qty=F('reserved_qty') - reservation.quantity,
                version=F('version') + 1
            )
            reservation.status = 'expired'
            await reservation.save()
            await StockTransaction.create(
                warehouse_id=reservation.warehouse_id,
                product_id=reservation.product_id,
                transaction_type='release',
                quantity=reservation.quantity,
                reference_id=reservation.order_id,
                created_by=SYSTEM_USER_ID
            )
```

[Ben Torres — 3:14 PM]
Looks clean. One edge case — what if the job is running and a customer completes their order at the same time? The `select_for_update` should prevent a race condition, but we should test it.

[Leah Kim — 3:14 PM]
Right. The `select_for_update` on the reservation row means the job can't process a reservation that the order-fulfillment path is simultaneously trying to fulfill. One of them will wait for the other's transaction to commit. If the fulfillment path commits first, the reservation status will be 'fulfilled', and the job's query won't match it. If the job commits first, the fulfillment path will see status='expired' and return an error to the customer, who would need to re-reserve. That's the correct behavior.

[Greg Holloway — 3:15 PM]
I'll add that race condition to the test matrix. Concurrent reservation expiry and order fulfillment — that's a good one.

[Omar Zafar — 3:15 PM]
This looks solid. The partitioning strategy for stock_levels and stock_transactions makes sense. Leah, go ahead with this schema. Let's review the indexing strategy in the next architecture meeting — Tuesday works?

[Leah Kim — 3:16 PM]
Tuesday works. I'll have the index proposals ready — covering indexes for the reservation lookup, partial indexes for active reservations, and the GIN index on the JSONB columns. I'll also have the migration scripts in a PR by Monday so people can review the DDL before we talk indexing.

[Fatima Al-Said — 3:17 PM]
One more thing — connection pooling. The legacy Oracle system uses 400 persistent connections. We're moving to microservices, so we'll have multiple service instances connecting. I'll set up PgBouncer in transaction mode between the application and Aurora. That way, even if we have 50 service instances, the database sees a bounded connection count.

[Leah Kim — 3:18 PM]
Good call. Transaction mode is the right choice for this workload. Session mode would tie a connection to a service instance for the lifetime of the process, which wastes connections. Transaction mode releases the connection back to the pool after each transaction, so we can serve many more concurrent requests with fewer database connections.

[Omar Zafar — 3:20 PM]

ITEM 3: INCIDENT REVIEW — STAGING REDIS CONNECTIVITY (10:52–11:10 AM)

[Carlos Rivera — 10:52 AM]
Minor incident to review. Yesterday at 4:17 PM, the staging session Redis became unreachable for 47 seconds. Here's what happened:

Root cause: ElastiCache performed an automatic failover test in the staging environment. This is a feature I enabled for staging to verify our failover behavior. The configuration is `--automatic-failover-enabled` with `test-failover` triggered via a scheduled CloudWatch event every Tuesday at 4 PM. During the failover, the primary node was replaced and the DNS endpoint updated. Our application's Redis connection pool had stale connections pointing to the old primary.

Timeline:
- 16:17:00 — ElastiCache initiates failover
- 16:17:02 — Primary node begins shutdown
- 16:17:15 — New primary promoted from replica
- 16:17:22 — DNS endpoint updated to new primary IP
- 16:17:47 — Application establishes new connections, service restored

Impact: During the 47-second window, session lookups in staging returned errors. The session middleware correctly fell back to the PostgreSQL backend for those requests — no user-visible impact. We saw 234 fallback events in the staging logs during that window.

The production Redis cluster has the same failover configuration but we haven't triggered it yet. Based on this staging test, I need to add connection health checking to our Redis pool — on connection error, discard the connection and establish a new one. The Python `redis-py` library supports this with the `health_check_interval` parameter, but we're using a custom connection pool wrapper that doesn't pass it through. I need to update the wrapper.

[Mike Torres — 10:56 AM]
The session middleware handled it correctly — it fell back to PostgreSQL. But once we remove the PostgreSQL fallback (Phase 2), a Redis outage would mean session lookups fail and users get 401s. How do we handle that?

[Carlos Rivera — 10:57 AM]
Two things. First, the connection health check I mentioned — after failover, new connections automatically point to the new primary via DNS. So the outage window is just the time it takes to establish new connections, not the full failover time. With health checking, that's sub-second. The `health_check_interval` parameter sends a PING before each command if the connection has been idle for more than N seconds. If the PING fails, it drops the connection and creates a new one.

Second, the ElastiCache Multi-AZ failover in production typically completes in 10-15 seconds. During that window, reads can go to the replica (I'll configure read replicas for session lookups). Writes (new session creation, token rotation) would fail briefly, but that only affects new logins and active rotations — existing sessions would get a stale token (old one still valid) and rotate on the next request.

[Sarah Kim — 10:58 AM]
Does the export worker also use the session Redis, or is it only the API service?

[Carlos Rivera — 10:58 AM]
Only the API service and the WebSocket service use the session Redis. The export worker authenticates via a service account token that's validated against the database directly — it doesn't go through the session middleware. So a session Redis outage wouldn't affect exports at all.

[Mike Torres — 10:59 AM]
That's acceptable. The worst case is a 15-second window where new logins fail and active users get a brief delay on token rotation. No data loss, no session invalidation.

[David Okafor — 11:00 AM]
For the cache Redis (the separate cluster for API caching), failure is even more benign — cache miss, database query, user sees normal latency instead of cached latency. No auth implications. And the cache Redis is a single-node setup without Multi-AZ, so its failure mode is just "node dies, we provision a new one, cache is cold." The application handles that gracefully because every cache read has a database fallback.

[Carlos Rivera — 11:01 AM]
Exactly. I want to stress again that these are separate clusters with separate failure domains. A session Redis failure does NOT affect cache Redis and vice versa. They're in different ElastiCache replication groups, different subnet groups, and the application uses different connection pool instances for each. There's no shared state between them.

[James Holden — 11:02 AM]
Should we document this failure mode in the incident runbook? It seems like a scenario the on-call person should know about.

[Carlos Rivera — 11:02 AM]

MEETING NOTES

[Omar Zafar — 10:02 AM]
Morning everyone. Looks like we have the full team plus a few extras today. Priya, Tomás, Wei — thanks for joining. This is the database selection review for WarehouseIQ telemetry storage. ADR-045. Hannah has done the evaluation and she'll walk us through the candidates. Hannah, can you set the context?

[Hannah Choi — 10:03 AM]
Sure. Let me share my screen — everyone see the slides? OK. So WarehouseIQ is our IoT platform. We have sensors in 340 warehouses monitoring temperature, humidity, door open/close events, forklift movement, and energy consumption. The sensors emit telemetry every 10 seconds.

Scale:
- 14,000 sensors across 340 warehouses
- Each sensor emits ~8,640 data points per day (every 10 seconds)
- Total: ~121 million data points per day
- Retention: 90 days of raw data, 2 years of hourly aggregates, 5 years of daily aggregates
- Raw data volume: 90 days × 121M points × ~200 bytes = ~2.2TB
- Query patterns: dashboard queries (time-range aggregations), alert evaluation (last-N-minutes checks), historical analysis (multi-month trends)

[Tomás Reyes — 10:06 AM]
Quick clarification on the 200 bytes per data point — is that the on-wire size or the storage size?

[Hannah Choi — 10:06 AM]
Storage size. The on-wire payload from the sensors is about 140 bytes, but with indexing overhead, metadata, and the JSON envelope that Elasticsearch wraps around each document, we end up at roughly 200 bytes stored per data point. That's the Elasticsearch number. The actual storage per row will vary by engine — TimescaleDB rows are denser, ClickHouse even more so after columnar compression.

[Tomás Reyes — 10:07 AM]
Got it. Makes sense.

[Hannah Choi — 10:07 AM]
We're currently storing this in Elasticsearch. It works but it's expensive — we're running a 9-node Elasticsearch cluster that costs $14,000/month. The cluster is overprovisioned for the write pattern (Elasticsearch is optimized for full-text search, not time-series aggregation) and underperforming on time-range queries (our P95 for a 24-hour temperature aggregation query is 3.2 seconds).

[Priya Narayanan — 10:08 AM]
And from the analytics perspective, that 3.2 seconds is the good case. When we run multi-week trends or cross-warehouse comparisons, it can take 15-20 seconds. Kibana dashboards time out regularly. My team has basically stopped using the Elasticsearch dashboards for historical analysis and started exporting CSVs instead, which is terrible for reproducibility.

[Hannah Choi — 10:09 AM]
Right. And that's exactly why we're here. I want to move to a purpose-built time-series database. I've evaluated three options and I want to present them.

[Omar Zafar — 10:10 AM]
Go ahead.

[Hannah Choi — 10:10 AM]
**Option 1: TimescaleDB**

TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension for time-series data. It adds "hypertables" — automatically partitioned tables where each partition (called a "chunk") covers a time range. Queries that specify a time range only scan the relevant chunks.

Pros:
- Full SQL support — our team knows SQL, our dashboards use SQL queries, no new query language
- PostgreSQL ecosystem — works with all PostgreSQL tools, extensions, and drivers
- Continuous aggregates — materialized views that auto-update as new data arrives. Perfect for our hourly/daily aggregation tiers
- Compression — 10-20x compression on historical chunks. Our 2.2TB raw data would compress to ~150GB
- We already chose PostgreSQL for InventoryHub v2 (ADR-042), so we have the operational muscle

Cons:
- Single-node performance ceiling. TimescaleDB's multi-node (distributed) was deprecated in favor of their cloud product. For our self-managed deployment, we're limited to what a single large instance can handle.
- Write throughput: benchmarked at 380K rows/sec on our test hardware. Our requirement is 14K rows/sec (121M points / 86,400 seconds). That's 27x headroom. Sufficient.
- Query performance: 24-hour temperature aggregation across 100 sensors — 180ms on compressed data. That's 17x faster than Elasticsearch's 3.2 seconds.

Cost estimate: Single db.r6g.2xlarge (8 vCPU, 64GB RAM) with 500GB gp3 EBS. Monthly cost: ~$1,200. That's $12,800/month savings versus Elasticsearch.

[Wei Zhang — 10:18 AM]
On the security side — since this is a PostgreSQL extension, we get standard PostgreSQL authentication, SSL/TLS, and row-level security if we ever need it. The sensor data isn't PII but some of the warehouse operational metrics could be commercially sensitive. PostgreSQL's permission model is well-understood and auditable.

[Hannah Choi — 10:19 AM]
Good point. That's another pro for TimescaleDB — no new security surface to evaluate.

[Hannah Choi — 10:19 AM]
**Option 2: InfluxDB 3.0**

InfluxDB is a dedicated time-series database. Version 3.0 is a ground-up rewrite using Apache Arrow and DataFusion for columnar storage and query execution.

Pros:
- Purpose-built for time-series — ingestion, storage, and query are all optimized for temporal data
- Columnar storage — extremely efficient for aggregation queries (only reads the columns needed)
- InfluxQL and SQL support — familiar query languages
- Built-in retention policies and downsampling — define rules to automatically aggregate and delete old data

Cons:
- InfluxDB 3.0 is relatively new (GA in early 2025). Fewer production deployments than TimescaleDB
- Operational unfamiliarity — nobody on the team has run InfluxDB in production
- Separate system — doesn't share infrastructure with our PostgreSQL stack
- Clustering requires InfluxDB Enterprise (paid) for high availability

[Tomás Reyes — 10:23 AM]
On the maturity point — I've been following the InfluxDB 3.0 release cycle. They had some rough edges in the early builds around WAL durability under heavy write load. Their changelog shows a fix in 3.0.3, but I haven't seen many production war stories yet. The InfluxDB 2.x community was solid, but 3.0 is architecturally different enough that prior operational experience doesn't fully transfer.

[Hannah Choi — 10:24 AM]
That's consistent with what I found. Their documentation is good but the community knowledge base is thin for 3.0 specifically.

[Hannah Choi — 10:24 AM]
Benchmark results on equivalent hardware:
- Write throughput: 520K rows/sec (higher than TimescaleDB due to columnar storage)
- 24-hour aggregation query: 95ms (faster than TimescaleDB for pure aggregation)
- Ad-hoc analytical query (multi-month trend with grouping): 340ms (comparable to TimescaleDB)

[Priya Narayanan — 10:25 AM]
What was the behavior like on unoptimized queries? Like, if someone does a SELECT * without a time range filter?

[Hannah Choi — 10:26 AM]
InfluxDB 3.0 handles it reasonably — it'll scan the full dataset but the columnar storage means it only reads the requested columns. A SELECT * without time range on 90 days of data took about 8 seconds for InfluxDB. TimescaleDB was similar at 9.5 seconds. ClickHouse was fastest at 3.2 seconds. But in all cases, the query planner warns you if you're doing a full table scan, so it's not silent.

[Priya Narayanan — 10:27 AM]
OK, that's acceptable. I'll make sure our team uses time range filters, but it's good to know the floor.

[Hannah Choi — 10:27 AM]
Cost estimate: 3-node InfluxDB Enterprise cluster with 500GB per node. InfluxDB Enterprise license: ~$3,000/month. Infrastructure: ~$1,800/month. Total: ~$4,800/month. Still $9,200 cheaper than Elasticsearch, but 4x more expensive than TimescaleDB.

[Jake Petrov — 10:28 AM]
Is there a community edition of InfluxDB 3.0 that supports clustering, or is Enterprise the only option for HA?

[Hannah Choi — 10:28 AM]
Enterprise is the only option for native clustering. The open-source edition is single-node only. You could theoretically build your own replication layer using write duplication at the application level, but that's custom infrastructure we'd have to build and maintain ourselves. Not worth it when TimescaleDB gives us HA through standard PostgreSQL streaming replication.

[Hannah Choi — 10:29 AM]
**Option 3: ClickHouse**

ClickHouse is a columnar analytics database. Not purpose-built for time-series but excellent at it due to columnar storage and vectorized query execution.

Pros:
- Extreme query performance — the fastest analytical query engine available. Our benchmark: 24-hour aggregation in 12ms. Multi-month trend in 85ms.
- Excellent compression — 15-30x on time-series data
- SQL support with extensions for time-series patterns (tumble, hop, session windows)
- Active open-source community, large company deployments (Uber, Cloudflare, eBay)

Cons:
- Not designed for high-frequency small writes. ClickHouse prefers batched inserts (1000+ rows per insert). Our sensors emit every 10 seconds — we'd need a write buffer (Kafka or in-memory) to batch writes.
- No built-in retention policies — we'd implement them as scheduled DROP PARTITION operations
- Updates and deletes are expensive (ClickHouse is append-optimized)
- Operational complexity — ClickHouse cluster management is non-trivial. ZooKeeper dependency for replication.

[Tomás Reyes — 10:33 AM]
I want to emphasize the ZooKeeper point. We deprecated our last ZooKeeper dependency when we moved off the old Kafka cluster in Q1. Bringing ZooKeeper back means we need to maintain a 3-node ZooKeeper ensemble plus the ClickHouse cluster itself. That's 6 nodes minimum for HA, plus the Kafka brokers for write buffering. We're looking at 9-10 nodes total versus 2 nodes for TimescaleDB with streaming replication.

[Fatima Al-Said — 10:34 AM]
Exactly. And each of those components has its own failure modes, its own monitoring, its own runbooks. The operational surface area is significantly larger.

[Hannah Choi — 10:34 AM]
Benchmark results:
- Write throughput: 1.2M rows/sec (with batch inserts of 10K rows)
- Single-row insert: 2ms (poor — not designed for this)
- 24-hour aggregation: 12ms (fastest by far)
- Multi-month trend: 85ms (fastest by far)

Cost estimate: 3-node ClickHouse cluster with Kafka write buffer. Infrastructure: ~$2,400/month. No licensing cost (open source). But operational cost is higher due to Kafka + ZooKeeper dependencies.

[Priya Narayanan — 10:35 AM]
The 12ms aggregation is stunning. But I want to ask — would we ever actually notice the difference between 12ms and 180ms in a dashboard context? Our dashboard polls every 30 seconds. Both are essentially instantaneous from the user's perspective.

[Hannah Choi — 10:36 AM]
For dashboards, no. You'd never notice. Where it matters is if we were running thousands of concurrent analytical queries — like a data science workload with hundreds of simultaneous notebook cells hitting the database. We're not doing that. Our concurrent query load peaks at about 50-60 dashboard panels refreshing simultaneously, plus maybe 5-10 ad-hoc queries from the analytics team.

[Omar Zafar — 10:37 AM]
Good analysis. Let me summarize the comparison:

| Criteria | TimescaleDB | InfluxDB 3.0 | ClickHouse |
|----------|-------------|--------------|------------|
| Monthly cost | $1,200 | $4,800 | $2,400 |
| Write throughput | 380K/s | 520K/s | 1.2M/s (batched) |
| Aggregation query | 180ms | 95ms | 12ms |
| SQL support | Full | Partial | Full |
| Team familiarity | High (PostgreSQL) | None | Low |
| Operational complexity | Low (single node) | Medium (cluster) | High (cluster + Kafka) |
| HA built-in | No (use RDS) | Yes (Enterprise) | Yes (with ZooKeeper) |

[Leah Kim — 10:39 AM]
From the InventoryHub perspective, using TimescaleDB has a strategic advantage: the IoT telemetry and the inventory data could potentially live in the same PostgreSQL instance. If a warehouse's temperature sensor shows a spike, we could join that data with the inventory table to identify which products are affected — all in one query.

[Nadia Okonkwo — 10:40 AM]
That's a compelling use case. The analytics team has been asking for exactly this — correlating environmental conditions with inventory quality metrics. Today it requires exporting data from Elasticsearch and joining it with PostgreSQL exports in a Python script. If both datasets are in PostgreSQL, it's a SQL JOIN.

[Priya Narayanan — 10:41 AM]
Nadia's right. The temperature-to-spoilage correlation analysis that the warehousing ops team wants — right now it takes my team about 4 hours to run because of the data export and reconciliation steps. If it's a single SQL query against a unified PostgreSQL instance, it's a 30-second Jupyter cell. That's a massive productivity gain for us. We've had that request sitting in our backlog for almost a year because the effort-to-value ratio was too high with the current architecture.

[Leah Kim — 10:42 AM]
And the InventoryHub PostgreSQL instance is already set up with the schema and access controls. Adding TimescaleDB hypertables alongside the inventory tables is architecturally clean — they're just tables with automatic partitioning. Same connection strings, same auth, same backup pipeline.

[Jake Petrov — 10:43 AM]
The ClickHouse query performance is impressive but the operational complexity is a deal-breaker for me. We'd be adding Kafka, ZooKeeper, and ClickHouse to our infrastructure stack. That's three new systems to manage, monitor, and maintain. For 14K writes/second, it's massive overkill.

[Hannah Choi — 10:44 AM]
Agreed. ClickHouse is designed for log analytics at massive scale — billions of events per day. We're at 121 million per day, which is large but not ClickHouse-large. It's like using a semi truck to deliver groceries.

[Tomás Reyes — 10:44 AM]

**Decision: We're going with TimescaleDB (as a PostgreSQL extension) on a self-managed EC2 instance for WarehouseIQ telemetry storage.**

Decision rationale:
1. Team familiarity — PostgreSQL expertise transfers directly
2. Cost — 91% reduction versus Elasticsearch ($1,200 vs $14,000/month)
3. Performance — 17x faster aggregation queries than Elasticsearch
4. Strategic alignment — potential for cross-domain SQL joins with InventoryHub data
5. Operational simplicity — single node, PostgreSQL tooling, no new infrastructure dependencies

Alternatives documented in ADR-045:
- InfluxDB 3.0: stronger native time-series features but higher cost and operational unfamiliarity
- ClickHouse: superior query performance but excessive operational complexity for our scale

Migration plan: Hannah owns. Phased approach — migrate 90 days of historical data from Elasticsearch, validate parity, switch ingest pipeline, decommission Elasticsearch cluster.

SCHEMA DESIGN PREVIEW (10:55 AM — 11:30 AM)

[Hannah Choi — 11:06 AM]
Quick preview of the schema. I'll do a full design review next week but I want to validate the hypertable structure. Let me share my screen again — here's the SQL.

```sql
-- Enable TimescaleDB extension
CREATE EXTENSION IF NOT EXISTS timescaledb;

-- Raw telemetry data
CREATE TABLE sensor_telemetry (
    time TIMESTAMPTZ NOT NULL,
    sensor_id UUID NOT NULL,
    warehouse_id UUID NOT NULL,
    sensor_type VARCHAR(30) NOT NULL,  -- temperature, humidity, door, forklift, energy
    value_numeric DOUBLE PRECISION,     -- for temperature, humidity, energy
    value_text VARCHAR(100),            -- for door (open/close), forklift (zone entry/exit)
    metadata JSONB                      -- sensor-specific additional data
);

-- Convert to hypertable with 1-day chunks
SELECT create_hypertable('sensor_telemetry', 'time',
    chunk_time_interval => INTERVAL '1 day'
);

-- Indexes
CREATE INDEX idx_telemetry_sensor_time ON sensor_telemetry (sensor_id, time DESC);
CREATE INDEX idx_telemetry_warehouse_type_time ON sensor_telemetry (warehouse_id, sensor_type, time DESC);

-- Compression policy: compress chunks older than 7 days
ALTER TABLE sensor_telemetry SET (
    timescaledb.compress,
    timescaledb.compress_segmentby = 'sensor_id, sensor_type',
    timescaledb.compress_orderby = 'time DESC'
);
SELECT add_compression_policy('sensor_telemetry', INTERVAL '7 days');

-- Retention policy: drop raw data older than 90 days
SELECT add_retention_policy('sensor_telemetry', INTERVAL '90 days');

-- Continuous aggregate: hourly averages (retained 2 years)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW sensor_hourly
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT
    time_bucket('1 hour', time) AS bucket,
    sensor_id,
    warehouse_id,
    sensor_type,
    AVG(value_numeric) AS avg_value,
    MIN(value_numeric) AS min_value,
    MAX(value_numeric) AS max_value,
    COUNT(*) AS sample_count
FROM sensor_telemetry
WHERE value_numeric IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY bucket, sensor_id, warehouse_id, sensor_type;

-- Refresh policy: refresh hourly aggregates every hour
SELECT add_continuous_aggregate_policy('sensor_hourly',
    start_offset => INTERVAL '3 hours',
    end_offset => INTERVAL '1 hour',
    schedule_interval => INTERVAL '1 hour'
);

-- Continuous aggregate: daily averages (retained 5 years)
CREATE MATERIALIZED VIEW sensor_daily
WITH (timescaledb.continuous) AS
SELECT
    time_bucket('1 day', bucket) AS bucket,
    sensor_id,
    warehouse_id,
    sensor_type,
    AVG(avg_value) AS avg_value,
    MIN(min_value) AS min_value,
    MAX(max_value) AS max_value,
    SUM(sample_count) AS sample_count
FROM sensor_hourly
GROUP BY time_bucket('1 day', bucket), sensor_id, warehouse_id, sensor_type;

SELECT add_continuous_aggregate_policy('sensor_daily',
    start_offset => INTERVAL '3 days',
    end_offset => INTERVAL '1 day',
    schedule_interval => INTERVAL '1 day'
);
```

[Nadia Okonkwo — 11:12 AM]

Having those pre-computed and automatically maintained means we don't need to build our own ETL rollup jobs, which is what we were planning to do with the Elasticsearch exports.

[Nadia Okonkwo — 11:13 AM]
Right. And the continuous aggregate refresh policies handle the edge cases — like late-arriving data or backfills. The 3-hour start_offset on the hourly policy means it re-aggregates the last 3 hours every time it runs, catching any late data points within that window.

The compression segmentby on sensor_id and sensor_type is smart — it means queries filtering on a specific sensor or sensor type only decompress the relevant segments. Most dashboard queries are per-warehouse-per-sensor-type, so this aligns with the access pattern.

[Hannah Choi — 11:14 AM]
Exactly. The segmentby tells the compressor to group rows by sensor_id and sensor_type before compressing. Each compressed segment contains data for one sensor of one type. When you query "give me the temperature for sensor X over the last 24 hours," TimescaleDB only decompresses the segments for sensor X of type temperature. Everything else stays compressed on disk.
